choices.choice_text: Raise the learning rate so the optimizer jumps out of memorized solutions.
choices.rationale: A higher learning rate mainly destabilizes optimization; it is not a targeted overfitting remedy. It may prevent convergence entirely rather than improving generalization on validation data.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A data scientist has only 2,000 labeled examples for a text classifier and finds that a single train/test split produces unstable metric estimates. She switches to 5-fold cross-validation. What does this technique primarily provide?

choices.choice_text: A lower-variance estimate of how the model will perform on unseen data
choices.rationale: Each fold serves as the validation set exactly once, and averaging the five scores yields a more stable estimate of out-of-sample performance. This is especially valuable when the dataset is too small for a large held-out test set.

choices.choice_text: A larger effective training set that directly increases the accuracy of the final deployed model
choices.rationale: This confuses evaluation with training: cross-validation is a performance-estimation protocol, and the k per-fold models are typically discarded. A final model is trained separately (often on all the data) once the evaluation is done.

choices.choice_text: An automated schedule for adjusting the learning rate across training epochs
choices.rationale: Learning-rate scheduling happens inside a single training run and is unrelated to resampling. Cross-validation scores can inform hyperparameter selection, but the procedure itself does not adjust anything during training.

choices.choice_text: A direct measure of training-set fit used to diagnose underfitting
choices.rationale: Cross-validation scores are computed on held-out folds the model never trained on, so they measure generalization. Underfitting is diagnosed from poor performance on the training data itself, not from validation folds.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A startup must ship a spam classifier this week using 4,000 labeled emails on CPU-only servers. An engineer proposes TF-IDF n-gram features with logistic regression instead of fine-tuning a transformer. Which advantage best justifies this choice?

choices.choice_text: It is cheap to train on CPU and remains competitive for small, keyword-driven classification tasks
choices.rationale: Sparse TF-IDF features with a linear model train in seconds on CPU and form a strong baseline for tasks like spam detection, where a modest set of discriminative terms carries most of the signal — a good fit for the deadline and hardware constraints.

choices.choice_text: TF-IDF vectors encode semantic similarity between synonyms more accurately than learned embeddings
choices.rationale: The opposite is true: TF-IDF treats each term as an independent dimension, so synonyms like 'cheap' and 'inexpensive' share no similarity. Learned embeddings place semantically related words near each other in vector space.

choices.choice_text: N-gram features capture long-range dependencies between words better than self-attention
choices.rationale: N-grams only capture local word order within a fixed window (e.g., 2-3 tokens). Self-attention was designed specifically to relate tokens across the entire sequence, so this reverses the actual trade-off.

choices.choice_text: TF-IDF weighting removes the need for labeled training data because term statistics are computed without supervision
choices.rationale: TF-IDF weights are indeed computed from term statistics without labels, but the logistic-regression classifier on top is still supervised and requires the labeled emails. Feature extraction being unsupervised does not make the task unsupervised.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An ML team must predict the exact dollar amount of each customer's next monthly cloud bill from historical usage data. A junior engineer proposes training a classifier. How should the problem be framed, and why?

choices.choice_text: Regression, because the target is a continuous numeric value rather than a discrete category
choices.rationale: A dollar amount is a continuous quantity, and regression models predict continuous outputs. The framing determines the appropriate output layer, loss function (e.g., MSE), and evaluation metrics.

choices.choice_text: Classification, because each distinct dollar amount can simply be treated as its own separate class label
choices.rationale: Discretizing a continuous target into thousands of classes destroys the ordinal and metric structure of the values (predicting $99 instead of $100 would be penalized like predicting $5), and most classes would have almost no training examples.

choices.choice_text: Clustering, because customers with similar spending patterns should be grouped together first
choices.rationale: Clustering is unsupervised and produces groups, not a numeric prediction per customer. Since a labeled target (the actual bill amount) exists, the task is supervised prediction, not exploratory grouping.

choices.choice_text: Classification, because regression models can only fit linear relationships in the data
choices.rationale: 'Regression' names the task of predicting continuous outputs, not the specific linear-regression model. Tree ensembles and neural networks routinely perform nonlinear regression.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An LLM team runs three stages: (1) next-token prediction on raw unlabeled web text; (2) instruction tuning on human-written prompt-response pairs; (3) optimizing the model against a reward model built from human preference rankings. Which learning paradigm does stage 3 primarily exemplify?

choices.choice_text: Reinforcement learning, because the policy is optimized to maximize a learned reward signal
choices.rationale: In RLHF-style alignment, the LLM acts as a policy whose generated outputs are scored by the reward model, and the policy is updated (e.g., with PPO) to increase expected reward — the defining loop of reinforcement learning.

choices.choice_text: Supervised learning, because humans provided the preference rankings used to build the reward model
choices.rationale: Training the reward model on preference rankings is a supervised sub-step, but stage 3 as described — optimizing the LLM against that reward signal — is reinforcement learning. This option conflates the two sub-steps.

choices.choice_text: Unsupervised learning, because no explicit labels are attached to the model's generated responses
choices.rationale: Stage 1 (next-token prediction on unlabeled text) is the self-supervised/unsupervised stage. Stage 3 has an explicit optimization signal — the reward — so it is not learning structure from unlabeled data.

choices.choice_text: Semi-supervised learning, because it combines labeled rankings with unlabeled generated text
choices.rationale: Semi-supervised learning augments a small labeled dataset with a large unlabeled one to improve the same prediction task. Stage 3 instead optimizes generated outputs against a reward function, which is a fundamentally different training mechanism.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A data scientist has already converted support-ticket text into numeric feature vectors and now needs to train a gradient-boosted decision-tree classifier to predict ticket escalations. Which Python library is purpose-built for this modeling step?

choices.choice_text: XGBoost
choices.rationale: XGBoost provides highly optimized gradient-boosted decision trees and is a standard, typically top-performing choice for classification on tabular numeric features like these vectors.

choices.choice_text: spaCy
choices.rationale: spaCy handles NLP preprocessing — tokenization, tagging, and named-entity recognition — which corresponds to the feature-extraction step already completed in this scenario. It does not train gradient-boosted tree models; its text-processing role ended once the vectors were produced.

choices.choice_text: Keras
choices.rationale: Keras is a high-level API for building and training neural networks. Gradient-boosted decision trees are a distinct tree-ensemble model family that Keras does not implement, even though both are 'ML training' libraries.

choices.choice_text: NetworkX
choices.rationale: NetworkX is for creating and analyzing graphs and networks. The 'trees' in gradient boosting are learned predictors, not graph datasets to analyze, so a graph-analytics library is the wrong tool despite the naming overlap.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A developer uses a zero-shot prompt to convert customer emails into a specific JSON layout. The model understands the task but frequently deviates from the required format. Which adjustment should the developer try first?

choices.choice_text: Fine-tune the model on a large labeled dataset of correctly formatted email outputs
choices.rationale: Fine-tuning can enforce output formats but is slow and costly, so it is a later resort. The misconception is that formatting problems require weight updates, when in-context examples usually resolve them.

choices.choice_text: Add two or three input-output examples to the prompt showing the exact layout
choices.rationale: Moving from zero-shot to few-shot is the cheapest, fastest first intervention for format-consistency problems: in-context examples demonstrate the desired output pattern directly, and the model can imitate the structure without any training.

choices.choice_text: Raise the sampling temperature so the model explores more output variations
choices.rationale: Higher temperature increases randomness in token selection, which makes format deviations more likely, not less. This reflects the misconception that temperature is a general quality knob.

choices.choice_text: Retrain the tokenizer so it handles JSON punctuation and braces more reliably
choices.rationale: The tokenizer is fixed alongside the pretrained model; replacing it would invalidate the learned embeddings. Tokenization is not the cause of prompt-level formatting inconsistency.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A prompt engineer adds step-by-step chain-of-thought instructions to several different workloads to improve accuracy. Which task type is most likely to show a measurable accuracy gain from this change?

choices.choice_text: Single-label sentiment classification of short, unambiguous product reviews
choices.rationale: Short-review sentiment is essentially single-step pattern recognition; adding reasoning steps mainly adds latency and tokens with little accuracy benefit. The misconception is that CoT improves every task uniformly.

choices.choice_text: Recalling a single well-known fact, such as a country's capital
choices.rationale: Simple factual recall is retrieved directly from the model's parametric knowledge in one step; reasoning aloud cannot create knowledge the model lacks and rarely changes the answer.

choices.choice_text: Multi-step math word problems that require intermediate calculations
choices.rationale: Chain-of-thought helps tasks that decompose into intermediate reasoning steps: verbalizing each step lets the model condition later tokens on earlier partial results, which typically improves multi-step arithmetic and logic.

choices.choice_text: Translating short phrases between two high-resource languages
choices.rationale: Short-phrase translation is a direct sequence-to-sequence mapping rather than multi-step reasoning, so chain-of-thought typically offers little improvement there.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team is building a support chatbot that must always answer in a formal tone, avoid discussing competitors, and follow company policy on every turn of the conversation. Where should these persistent instructions be placed?

choices.choice_text: In the temperature and top-p settings, which control the model's tone and topic scope
choices.rationale: Sampling parameters control randomness in token selection, not persona, tone, or topical restrictions. Conflating decoding settings with behavioral instructions is a common confusion.

choices.choice_text: Repeated inside every user message, because the model only follows its most recent instruction
choices.rationale: Instructions can appear in user messages, but repeating them each turn is redundant and less reliable. The misconception is that models ignore everything except the latest message; the system prompt persists in context.

choices.choice_text: In the fine-tuning data only, since prompts cannot shape a model's persona
choices.rationale: Fine-tuning can shape persona but is not required for it; system prompts are the standard lightweight way to set behavior, so claiming prompts cannot influence persona is incorrect.

choices.choice_text: In the system prompt, which frames the model's behavior for the entire session
choices.rationale: The system prompt is the mechanism designed to establish persistent role, tone, and constraints that apply across all turns, and chat models are typically trained to weight it heavily.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer rewrote a prompt and observed better answers on three hand-picked queries. Before adopting the new prompt in production, which practice best reflects a disciplined, empirical prompt-engineering process?

choices.choice_text: Compare both prompt versions against a fixed set of representative test cases
choices.rationale: Prompt engineering is an empirical, iterative process: evaluating each single change on a consistent, representative test set isolates its effect and guards against overfitting to a few favorable examples.

choices.choice_text: Ship the new prompt immediately, since the three improved outputs confirm it is better
choices.rationale: Three cherry-picked queries are too small and unrepresentative a sample; apparent gains may not generalize. The misconception is treating anecdotal wins as sufficient validation.

choices.choice_text: Bundle several additional prompt edits into the same release to maximize the total gain
choices.rationale: Changing multiple prompt elements at once confounds the results, making it impossible to attribute any improvement or regression to a specific edit. Disciplined iteration changes one variable at a time.

choices.choice_text: Ask a colleague which single sample output they prefer and adopt that version
choices.rationale: One subjective preference on one output is anecdotal evidence; a systematic comparison over many representative cases is needed before adopting a change.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A user places five labeled examples in a prompt, and the model then classifies new inputs correctly. The user asks whether the model has now permanently learned these labels. What actually happened during this in-context learning?

choices.choice_text: It applied a handful of small gradient-descent updates to its attention weights while reading the examples
choices.rationale: No gradients are computed or applied during inference; a forward pass never modifies weights. The misconception is equating in-context adaptation with actual training updates.

choices.choice_text: Its weights stayed fixed; the examples steer outputs only while they remain in context
choices.rationale: In-context learning is inference-time conditioning: the frozen parameters compute over whatever is in the prompt, and once the examples fall out of the context window their influence ends.

choices.choice_text: It wrote the new label mappings into its parameters, so they persist across future sessions
choices.rationale: Parameters change only through training procedures such as fine-tuning; a prompt cannot write information into model weights, so nothing carries over to a fresh session.

choices.choice_text: It stored the examples in its embedding table and will retrieve them for later queries
choices.rationale: The embedding table is a learned parameter matrix that is fixed at inference time; models have no built-in persistent store unless an external system such as retrieval-augmented generation is added.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An ML team has a pre-trained base LLM and wants to align it with human preferences using the standard RLHF pipeline. In what order should they run the three stages?

choices.choice_text: Reward model training, then supervised fine-tuning, then RL policy optimization
choices.rationale: This reflects the misconception that the reward model comes first. In the standard pipeline the reward model is trained on comparisons between responses sampled from the supervised fine-tuned model, so SFT precedes reward model training.

choices.choice_text: Supervised fine-tuning, then reward model training, then RL policy optimization
choices.rationale: Correct. RLHF first uses supervised fine-tuning on demonstrations to give the model a reasonable instruction-following policy, then trains a reward model on human preference comparisons of that policy's outputs, and finally optimizes the policy with RL (e.g., PPO) against the reward model.

choices.choice_text: RL policy optimization, then supervised fine-tuning, then reward model training
choices.rationale: RL cannot run first because it requires a reward signal, which does not exist until a reward model has been trained. Placing SFT and reward modeling after RL leaves the RL stage with nothing to optimize against.

choices.choice_text: Supervised fine-tuning, then RL policy optimization, then reward model training
choices.rationale: This gets SFT in the right place but swaps the last two stages. The RL stage maximizes scores produced by the reward model, so the reward model must be trained before, not after, policy optimization.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer is preparing the training data for the reward model stage of an RLHF pipeline. Which kind of data should this stage use?

choices.choice_text: A large unlabeled text corpus used for next-token prediction
choices.rationale: This confuses reward modeling with pre-training. Unlabeled corpora teach general language modeling via next-token prediction; they carry no signal about which responses humans prefer.

choices.choice_text: Human-written prompt and response demonstration pairs
choices.rationale: This is the data for the supervised fine-tuning stage, not the reward model. Demonstrations show one good answer per prompt, whereas the reward model needs relative judgments between alternative answers.

choices.choice_text: Human preference comparisons ranking pairs of candidate model responses
choices.rationale: Correct. The reward model is trained on comparison data where humans indicate which of two (or more) responses to the same prompt they prefer, learning a scalar score that reflects those preferences.

choices.choice_text: Automated metric scores such as BLEU computed against reference texts
choices.rationale: This assumes the reward signal comes from n-gram overlap metrics. Such metrics require references and correlate poorly with human preference on open-ended generation, which is exactly why RLHF learns a reward model from human comparisons instead.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team finds PPO-based RLHF too complex and unstable to maintain, so they switch to Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Which components of the RLHF pipeline does DPO remove?

choices.choice_text: The separately trained reward model and the RL optimization loop
choices.rationale: Correct. DPO reformulates preference alignment as a direct classification-style loss on preference pairs, so no explicit reward model is trained and no RL algorithm such as PPO is needed.

choices.choice_text: The need to collect human preference data over response pairs
choices.rationale: This misreads 'direct' as removing human input. DPO still trains on the same chosen-versus-rejected preference pairs; it only changes how that data is optimized.

choices.choice_text: The reference model used to constrain divergence from the starting policy
choices.rationale: DPO's loss still uses a frozen reference policy, with the beta coefficient controlling how strongly the trained policy is kept close to it — the same role the KL penalty plays in PPO-based RLHF. The reference model is retained, not removed.

choices.choice_text: The supervised fine-tuning stage that precedes preference optimization
choices.rationale: DPO is typically still applied on top of a supervised fine-tuned model, which also serves as its reference policy. Skipping SFT is not what DPO simplifies away.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A colleague says instruction tuning and RLHF are interchangeable terms for the same alignment step. Which statement best captures the actual distinction?

choices.choice_text: Instruction tuning changes only lightweight adapter weights, while RLHF is the only method that updates the full network's parameters end to end
choices.rationale: This conflates the alignment objective with parameter-efficient techniques. Either stage can be run with full fine-tuning or with adapters such as LoRA; the parameter footprint is not what distinguishes them.

choices.choice_text: Instruction tuning happens during pre-training on raw text, while RLHF is the only stage that occurs after pre-training completes
choices.rationale: Both instruction tuning and RLHF are post-training steps applied after pre-training. Pre-training uses self-supervised next-token prediction on raw corpora, not instruction data.

choices.choice_text: Instruction tuning relies on human-ranked response comparisons, while RLHF relies only on model-generated synthetic labels
choices.rationale: This reverses the data types. Ranked comparisons are the input to RLHF's reward model, while instruction tuning uses demonstration pairs; and RLHF's canonical form uses human feedback, not purely synthetic labels.

choices.choice_text: Instruction tuning is supervised learning on prompt-response demonstrations, while RLHF optimizes against a reward learned from preference comparisons
choices.rationale: Correct. Instruction tuning imitates human-written example responses with a standard supervised loss, whereas RLHF goes further by training a reward model on preference rankings and then optimizing the policy against that reward.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A developer raises the sampling temperature from 0.7 to 1.5 and notices the model's outputs become more varied and surprising. What does the temperature parameter actually change?

choices.choice_text: It restricts sampling to the k highest-probability tokens at each decoding step
choices.rationale: This describes top-k sampling, a separate decoding control. Temperature reshapes the probabilities of all tokens rather than cutting the candidate set to a fixed count.

choices.choice_text: It rescales the logits before softmax, flattening or sharpening the token probability distribution
choices.rationale: Correct. Logits are divided by the temperature before the softmax: values above 1 flatten the distribution so low-probability tokens are sampled more often, while values below 1 sharpen it toward the most likely tokens.

choices.choice_text: It truncates the candidate pool at a cumulative probability threshold before sampling
choices.rationale: This describes top-p (nucleus) sampling. Temperature does not remove tokens from consideration; it only changes how probability mass is spread across them.

choices.choice_text: It applies a penalty that lowers the probability of tokens the model has already generated
choices.rationale: This describes a repetition or frequency penalty. Temperature is applied uniformly to the logits at each step and has no memory of which tokens appeared earlier in the output.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Your team is budgeting a series of progressively larger LLM pre-training runs. Before committing GPUs, a colleague asks what neural scaling laws actually let you forecast in advance. Which quantity do scaling laws predict most reliably as model size, dataset size, and compute increase?

choices.choice_text: Held-out cross-entropy loss, which decreases smoothly as a power law
choices.rationale: Empirical scaling laws (Kaplan et al., Hoffmann et al.) show test loss falls along smooth power-law curves in parameters, data, and compute, allowing forecasts across orders of magnitude of scale.

choices.choice_text: The specific downstream tasks on which new emergent capabilities will suddenly appear
choices.rationale: Emergent, task-specific abilities are notoriously hard to anticipate; scaling laws describe aggregate loss trends, not which discrete capabilities will appear at a given scale.

choices.choice_text: Wall-clock training time per optimizer step on a given GPU cluster
choices.rationale: Step time depends on hardware, parallelism strategy, and implementation efficiency; scaling laws relate loss to scale, not to engineering throughput on specific clusters.

choices.choice_text: Factual accuracy on individual benchmark questions in specialized domains
choices.rationale: Per-question accuracy is discrete and task-dependent; a smooth decline in average loss does not translate into reliable predictions for any single benchmark item.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Your team has a fixed pre-training compute budget. The initial plan maximizes parameter count, training a very large model on relatively few tokens. Based on the Chinchilla compute-optimal findings, which change would most likely produce a better model at the same compute cost?

choices.choice_text: Shrink the model and train on proportionally more tokens so parameters and data stay balanced
choices.rationale: Chinchilla showed many earlier LLMs were substantially undertrained: at fixed compute, loss is minimized when parameters and training tokens are kept roughly in balance (about 20 tokens per parameter), so a smaller model trained on more data outperforms an oversized, data-starved one.

choices.choice_text: Keep the large model but lower the learning rate to compensate for the limited token count
choices.rationale: Learning-rate tuning does not fix the underlying parameter-data imbalance; the model remains undertrained relative to its capacity regardless of the schedule.

choices.choice_text: Grow the model even further, because capability is driven almost entirely by parameter count
choices.rationale: This reflects the pre-Chinchilla emphasis on model size; Chinchilla's result was precisely that data must scale alongside parameters, so growing the model further worsens the compute-optimality gap.

choices.choice_text: Keep the large model and repeat the small dataset for many additional epochs
choices.rationale: Repeating a limited corpus yields diminishing returns compared with fresh tokens and can degrade quality; it is not a substitute for scaling the amount of unique training data.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An architect proposes replacing dense feed-forward layers with a Mixture-of-Experts design so the model's total parameter count can grow substantially without a matching rise in compute per token. Which property of MoE enables this?

choices.choice_text: A learned router selects a few experts per token, so only a fraction of parameters is active
choices.rationale: Sparse top-k gating means each token's forward pass touches only the selected experts, so FLOPs per token track the active parameters rather than the total parameter count.

choices.choice_text: Every expert processes every token in parallel, and their outputs are then averaged, keeping per-token computation constant
choices.rationale: If all experts ran on every token, compute would scale with the number of experts — that describes a dense ensemble and would defeat the efficiency goal of MoE.

choices.choice_text: The experts share a single set of weights, which keeps the effective parameter count small
choices.rationale: MoE experts have distinct weights; sharing them would eliminate the extra capacity that motivates the design. The point is high total capacity with low active compute.

choices.choice_text: Experts are stored at lower numerical precision than the shared attention layers throughout
choices.rationale: Quantization is an orthogonal optimization technique; MoE's compute advantage comes from sparse activation via routing, not from reduced numerical precision.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A product team is evaluating a generative model for a text-to-image feature. The candidate model produces an image by starting from pure random noise and refining it over many successive steps. Which model family and mechanism does this describe?

choices.choice_text: A diffusion model, which learns to reverse a gradual noising process via iterative denoising
choices.rationale: Diffusion models train a network to remove noise added by a forward noising process; generation runs this denoiser iteratively from pure noise, typically conditioned on a text prompt.

choices.choice_text: A GAN, which refines noise across many steps using a discriminator's feedback at inference time
choices.rationale: GANs generate an image in a single generator forward pass; the discriminator provides an adversarial training signal only and plays no iterative role at inference.

choices.choice_text: An autoregressive transformer, which removes noise from the image one pixel position at a time
choices.rationale: Autoregressive image models generate pixels or image tokens sequentially by next-element prediction; they do not operate by denoising a full noisy canvas.

choices.choice_text: A variational autoencoder, which sharpens outputs by repeatedly re-encoding its latent representation
choices.rationale: A VAE decodes a sampled latent vector in one pass; it does not iteratively refine images by cycling through the encoder, and repeated re-encoding is not its generation mechanism.

mcqQuestions.question_text: You must build a classifier for legal-domain documents but have only a few thousand labeled examples. A colleague suggests fine-tuning a pretrained language model rather than training a model from scratch. What is the primary reason this approach typically performs better?

choices.choice_text: Pre-training already learned general language representations, so fine-tuning needs far less labeled data
choices.rationale: Transfer learning reuses syntactic and semantic features learned from massive unlabeled corpora, so the small labeled set only has to teach the task-specific mapping, cutting data and compute requirements substantially.

choices.choice_text: Fine-tuning updates only the final classification layer, which makes overfitting on small datasets impossible
choices.rationale: Fine-tuning commonly updates many or all weights, and even head-only tuning can still overfit a small dataset; the claim that overfitting becomes impossible is the misconception.

choices.choice_text: The pretrained model has already memorized the target labels during its pre-training phase
choices.rationale: Self-supervised pre-training uses objectives like next-token or masked-token prediction on unlabeled text; the model has never seen the downstream task's labels.

choices.choice_text: Training from scratch requires a fundamentally different architecture than fine-tuning does
choices.rationale: The same architecture can be trained either way; fine-tuning simply continues optimization from pretrained weights, usually with a small task head added, rather than from random initialization.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer serves a text classifier on Triton Inference Server. Clients send single requests one at a time, and GPU utilization stays low. She enables dynamic batching in the model configuration. What does this feature do?

choices.choice_text: Combines queued individual requests into larger server-side batches, raising GPU utilization and throughput.
choices.rationale: Dynamic batching holds incoming requests for a short, configurable delay and merges them into a single batch before execution, so the GPU processes many requests per kernel launch and throughput improves.

choices.choice_text: Splits oversized client batches into smaller chunks so each fits within available GPU memory.
choices.rationale: This reverses the feature: dynamic batching aggregates small requests into bigger batches. Batch size limits are enforced by max_batch_size, and splitting is not what the feature is for.

choices.choice_text: Compresses request payloads before network transfer so inputs reach the server with lower latency.
choices.rationale: This confuses batching with transport optimization. Dynamic batching operates on requests already queued at the server; it does not change how payloads are encoded or transmitted.

choices.choice_text: Caches responses to repeated identical requests so duplicate inferences skip GPU execution entirely.
choices.rationale: That describes Triton's separate response cache feature. Dynamic batching still executes every request; it just groups them for more efficient GPU execution.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Profiling shows a single instance of an ONNX model leaves an A100 GPU underutilized while requests queue, and latency headroom exists. Which change to the model's Triton Inference Server configuration raises throughput on that one GPU without modifying the model itself?

choices.choice_text: Set the instance_group count above one so Triton runs multiple model instances concurrently.
choices.rationale: Triton's instance groups let several copies of the same model execute concurrently on one GPU, and its scheduler overlaps their work, increasing utilization and throughput with no change to the model.

choices.choice_text: Enable an ensemble configuration, which replicates the model and load-balances requests across replicas.
choices.rationale: Ensembles chain different models into a server-side pipeline (e.g., preprocess, infer, postprocess); they do not replicate one model for parallel execution. Replication is what instance groups provide.

choices.choice_text: Launch a second Triton server process on the same GPU and place a load balancer in front.
choices.rationale: This is not a model configuration change, and it adds unnecessary complexity: a single Triton server already schedules multiple model instances on one GPU natively through instance groups.

choices.choice_text: Rebuild the model with INT8 quantization so each request completes faster on the GPU.
choices.rationale: Quantization can reduce per-request latency but it changes the model, which the scenario rules out, and it does not address the core problem of only one instance executing at a time.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A developer adds a model to a Triton model repository and writes a config.pbtxt file beside the model artifact. What does this configuration file primarily control?

choices.choice_text: Serving properties such as the backend, max batch size, tensor shapes and datatypes, and instance count.
choices.rationale: config.pbtxt declares how Triton should serve the model: which backend/platform runs it, input and output tensor specifications, max_batch_size, instance groups, and scheduling options like dynamic batching.

choices.choice_text: The trained weights and layer parameters that Triton must load into GPU memory when the model starts.
choices.rationale: Weights live in the model artifact itself (e.g., a .onnx or .plan file inside the version directory). The config describes serving behavior, not learned parameters.

choices.choice_text: Training hyperparameters such as learning rate, optimizer choice, and number of epochs.
choices.rationale: Triton is an inference server, so training hyperparameters are irrelevant to it. This confuses a serving configuration with a training recipe such as those used in NeMo.

choices.choice_text: Cluster deployment settings such as replica counts, CPU limits, and container image versions.
choices.rationale: Those settings belong to orchestration manifests like Kubernetes or Helm charts. config.pbtxt configures one model within a Triton server, not the infrastructure the server runs on.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A PyTorch model runs behind a hand-built FastAPI endpoint. Traffic grows, the GPU idles between calls, and the team now needs request batching, metrics, and an additional ONNX model. Why is Triton Inference Server a stronger choice than extending the FastAPI wrapper?

choices.choice_text: Triton supplies dynamic batching, multi-framework backends, concurrent execution, and metrics without custom code.
choices.rationale: These are exactly the production serving features the team would otherwise build by hand: Triton batches requests, hosts PyTorch and ONNX models side by side, runs them concurrently, and exports Prometheus metrics out of the box.

choices.choice_text: Triton compiles each model into fused GPU kernels, removing the framework overhead a Python server incurs.
choices.rationale: Kernel fusion and compilation are TensorRT's job, not Triton's. Triton executes models through backends; it does not compile arbitrary models into optimized kernels itself.

choices.choice_text: FastAPI cannot invoke GPU inference, so any CUDA-backed model requires a dedicated inference server.
choices.rationale: A Python web app can call GPU-backed models directly, which is exactly what the prototype does. The gap is missing serving features like batching and scheduling, not GPU access.

choices.choice_text: Triton monitors prediction accuracy and automatically retrains models when data drift is detected.
choices.rationale: Triton is an inference server and performs no training or retraining. Drift monitoring and retraining pipelines are separate MLOps concerns outside Triton's scope.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team targets minimal latency for a transformer model in production and plans to use TensorRT together with Triton Inference Server. Which division of labor between the two tools is correct?

choices.choice_text: TensorRT compiles the model into an optimized engine; Triton serves that engine, handling requests and batching.
choices.rationale: TensorRT is the offline optimizer: it applies layer fusion, precision tuning, and kernel selection to produce an engine. Triton then hosts the engine, exposing endpoints and managing scheduling and batching.

choices.choice_text: Triton performs the kernel fusion and precision calibration; TensorRT then exposes HTTP and gRPC endpoints to client applications.
choices.rationale: This reverses the roles. Graph optimization and calibration are TensorRT capabilities, while networked endpoints, request handling, and scheduling belong to Triton.

choices.choice_text: TensorRT both optimizes and serves the model; Triton is needed only when scaling across multiple nodes.
choices.rationale: TensorRT is an optimization library and runtime, not a networked server; it has no client-facing serving layer. Triton adds value even on a single node through endpoints, batching, and scheduling.

choices.choice_text: Triton retrains the model for the target GPU; TensorRT quantizes the retrained weights for deployment.
choices.rationale: Neither tool trains models: Triton serves them and TensorRT optimizes them for inference. Quantization is one step inside TensorRT's engine build, not a stage that follows retraining by Triton.

mcqQuestions.question_text: During a design review, a teammate attributes several capabilities to the wrong NVIDIA tool. Which two capabilities belong to Triton Inference Server rather than TensorRT or NeMo? (Choose two.)

choices.choice_text: Serving models from multiple frameworks, such as PyTorch, ONNX, and TensorRT, behind common HTTP/gRPC endpoints.
choices.rationale: Triton's backend architecture is exactly this: it hosts models from many frameworks side by side and exposes them through unified HTTP and gRPC APIs.

choices.choice_text: Running several instances of a model concurrently on a shared GPU with a built-in request scheduler.
choices.rationale: Concurrent model execution via instance groups and Triton's scheduler is a core Triton serving feature that raises GPU utilization; neither TensorRT nor NeMo manages live request scheduling.

choices.choice_text: Fusing layers and calibrating INT8 precision to compile a network into an optimized inference engine.
choices.rationale: Layer fusion and INT8 calibration are engine-building optimizations performed by TensorRT ahead of deployment. Triton can serve the resulting engine but does not perform the compilation.

choices.choice_text: Providing training recipes and parameter-efficient fine-tuning workflows for large language models.
choices.rationale: Curated training and PEFT/fine-tuning workflows are NeMo framework capabilities. Triton is inference-only and plays no role in training or adaptation.

choices.choice_text: Converting a trained network into a hardware-specific serialized engine file before deployment.
choices.rationale: Producing serialized, GPU-specific engine files (e.g., .plan files) is what TensorRT does during its build step. Triton consumes such artifacts at serving time rather than creating them.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A TensorRT engineer inspects a built engine and sees that a GEMM, bias add, and GELU activation were fused into a single kernel. What is the primary mechanism by which this fusion reduces inference latency?

choices.choice_text: It reduces the parameter count by merging the weights of adjacent layers into a single smaller tensor.
choices.rationale: This confuses kernel fusion with model compression such as pruning. Fusion changes how operations execute, not how many weights the model has — the mathematical computation and parameters are unchanged.

choices.choice_text: It reduces kernel launch overhead and keeps intermediates on-chip rather than in global memory.
choices.rationale: Correct. Fusion replaces several kernel launches with one and lets intermediate results stay in registers or shared memory, avoiding costly writes to and re-reads from global GPU memory. Since many of these ops are memory-bandwidth-bound, cutting that traffic is the main latency win.

choices.choice_text: It lowers the numerical precision of the fused operations so they execute with fewer bits.
choices.rationale: This confuses fusion with quantization or reduced-precision execution. Fusion by itself preserves the configured precision; precision reduction (FP16/INT8) is a separate TensorRT optimization applied independently of fusion.

choices.choice_text: It splits the fused operations across multiple GPUs so they execute concurrently.
choices.rationale: This confuses fusion with model or tensor parallelism. Fusion combines ops into one kernel on a single device; distributing work across GPUs is a different technique with its own communication costs.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team applies post-training INT8 quantization to a model using a representative calibration dataset, but task accuracy drops well below acceptable limits. They must keep INT8 inference for its latency and memory benefits. Which approach most directly recovers accuracy?

choices.choice_text: Collect a much larger calibration dataset and re-run post-training quantization.
choices.rationale: The misconception is that calibration data volume alone closes any accuracy gap. Calibration only tunes activation ranges; if the model's weights are inherently sensitive to INT8 precision, more calibration samples cannot make the weights adapt — only training can.

choices.choice_text: Switch the deployment to FP16 precision, which provides the same latency and memory savings as INT8.
choices.rationale: FP16 usually preserves accuracy but uses twice the bits of INT8, so it does not deliver the same memory footprint or throughput on INT8-capable hardware. It also abandons the stated requirement to keep INT8 inference.

choices.choice_text: Fine-tune with quantization-aware training so the weights adapt to simulated INT8 effects.
choices.rationale: Correct. QAT inserts fake-quantization ops into the forward pass during fine-tuning, so the model learns weights that are robust to INT8 rounding and clipping. This typically recovers accuracy close to the FP32 baseline while still deploying at INT8.

choices.choice_text: Prune low-magnitude weights before quantizing to make the model easier to calibrate.
choices.rationale: This confuses two separate compression techniques. Pruning removes weights to shrink the model and, without retraining, tends to add its own accuracy loss on top of quantization error rather than recovering it.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team already serves a transformer with a generic TensorRT engine but struggles with many concurrent generation requests of varying lengths and with growing KV-cache memory. Why would migrating to TensorRT-LLM help?

choices.choice_text: It runs model checkpoints as-is, with no engine building or kernel optimization involved.
choices.rationale: The misconception is that TensorRT-LLM skips optimization entirely. Its benefit comes precisely from LLM-specific optimized kernels compiled for the target GPU plus a specialized serving runtime — checkpoints are not simply executed unoptimized.

choices.choice_text: It fine-tunes the model during deployment so responses become shorter and cheaper to generate.
choices.rationale: This confuses an inference-optimization library with training. TensorRT-LLM does not modify or fine-tune model weights; it optimizes how a fixed model executes and how requests are scheduled.

choices.choice_text: It offloads the entire KV cache to CPU memory by default, eliminating GPU memory pressure.
choices.rationale: The core differentiator is paged management of the KV cache in GPU memory, not wholesale CPU offload. Routing every attention read through host memory by default would add severe latency, and offloading does not remove GPU memory pressure for active sequences.

choices.choice_text: It provides in-flight batching and paged KV-cache management designed for LLM serving.
choices.rationale: Correct. TensorRT-LLM extends TensorRT with an LLM-specific runtime: in-flight (continuous) batching lets new requests join and finished ones leave a running batch, and paged KV-cache allocation manages attention memory in blocks — directly addressing variable-length concurrency and KV-cache growth.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A research group trains a model in PyTorch and wants production teams to optimize and run it with TensorRT and other runtimes without rewriting the model code. What role does exporting to ONNX play in this workflow?

choices.choice_text: It expresses the model as a framework-neutral operator graph that many runtimes can consume.
choices.rationale: Correct. ONNX is an open interchange format: the trained model becomes a graph of standardized operators, decoupling it from PyTorch. Tools like TensorRT's ONNX parser or ONNX Runtime can then ingest and optimize that graph, solving the framework-to-runtime interoperability problem.

choices.choice_text: It compiles the model directly into optimized GPU kernels, so no further optimization step is needed.
choices.rationale: The misconception is that ONNX is itself a compiler or runtime. ONNX is only a model description format; kernel selection and optimization happen later in an engine such as TensorRT or ONNX Runtime.

choices.choice_text: It automatically quantizes the model to INT8 as part of the export process.
choices.rationale: This confuses a serialization format with a precision optimization. Exporting to ONNX preserves the model's existing precision; quantization is a separate step performed by downstream tools if desired.

choices.choice_text: It retrains the model inside the target framework to reproduce its original behavior.
choices.rationale: The misconception is that conversion requires retraining. ONNX export traces or scripts the already-trained computation graph and copies the existing weights; no additional training occurs.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team serializes a TensorRT engine after optimizing an LLM on Ampere-generation GPUs in staging. Which change in production requires building a new engine rather than reusing the serialized one?

choices.choice_text: Sending prompts with different text content that fit the configured shape profile.
choices.rationale: The misconception is that engines are tied to specific inputs. Input content can vary freely at runtime as long as the tensor shapes fall within the engine's optimization profile; no rebuild is needed.

choices.choice_text: Restarting the inference server process that loads the engine.
choices.rationale: The misconception is that engines are ephemeral to a process. A serialized engine is a persistent artifact on disk; a restarted server simply deserializes and reuses the same file.

choices.choice_text: Deploying onto GPUs of a different architecture generation, such as Hopper.
choices.rationale: Correct. A TensorRT engine is compiled with kernels selected and tuned for a specific GPU architecture (and TensorRT version), so an Ampere-built engine is not portable to a Hopper GPU — the engine must be rebuilt on or for the new target. Precision or shape-profile changes similarly require a rebuild.

choices.choice_text: Adding more replicas of the engine on identical Ampere GPUs.
choices.rationale: The misconception is that each instance needs its own build. The same serialized engine can be loaded by multiple replicas on matching GPU hardware, which is the standard way to scale out serving.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A developer beginning an LLM project wants GPU-optimized framework containers, pretrained model checkpoints, and Helm charts for deployment, all downloadable from a single NVIDIA-hosted hub. Which resource provides these?

choices.choice_text: NGC Catalog
choices.rationale: The NGC Catalog is NVIDIA's curated hub of GPU-optimized containers, pretrained models, Helm charts, and SDKs, exactly matching the developer's need for downloadable software and model artifacts.

choices.choice_text: NVIDIA Base Command
choices.rationale: This confuses the artifact hub with the workload-management layer. Base Command schedules and monitors training jobs on DGX infrastructure; it pulls containers from NGC rather than distributing them.

choices.choice_text: DGX Cloud
choices.rationale: This mistakes a compute service for a software catalog. DGX Cloud rents managed AI supercomputing capacity; it is where you run workloads, not where you download containers and models.

choices.choice_text: NVIDIA AI Enterprise
choices.rationale: This conflates the support license with the distribution channel. AI Enterprise adds enterprise support and validation for NVIDIA software, but the downloadable containers and models themselves live in the NGC Catalog.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An ML team shares one A100 GPU across several small inference services and needs each service to receive dedicated memory and compute with hardware-level fault isolation. Which GPU capability meets this requirement?

choices.choice_text: Multi-Instance GPU (MIG)
choices.rationale: MIG partitions a supported GPU into up to seven instances, each with its own dedicated memory, compute slices, and hardware-level isolation, so one service cannot starve or crash another.

choices.choice_text: CUDA stream concurrency
choices.rationale: This confuses kernel-level concurrency with partitioning. Streams let kernels overlap on the same GPU but share memory and compute without any isolation or per-service resource caps.

choices.choice_text: Time-slicing the GPU across processes
choices.rationale: This mistakes temporal sharing for isolation. Time-slicing alternates whole-GPU access between processes, offering no memory partitioning, no quality-of-service guarantee, and no fault isolation between tenants.

choices.choice_text: Tensor parallelism
choices.rationale: This inverts the scenario. Tensor parallelism splits one large model across multiple GPUs to fit or speed it up; it does not divide a single GPU among independent workloads.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A regulated company already runs free NGC containers in production. Management asks what an NVIDIA AI Enterprise subscription would add on top of the free software. What is the primary benefit of the license?

choices.choice_text: Enterprise support with SLAs, security patching, and a validated software stack
choices.rationale: NVIDIA AI Enterprise licenses a supported, security-maintained, and validated version of the NVIDIA AI software stack, certified on mainstream servers and virtualization platforms, which is what regulated production environments typically require.

choices.choice_text: Paid access to CUDA and cuDNN, which are otherwise unlicensed for business use
choices.rationale: This reflects the misconception that core NVIDIA libraries require a paid license. CUDA and cuDNN are freely downloadable and usable commercially; the subscription is about support and validation, not library access.

choices.choice_text: Firmware unlocks that raise GPU clock speeds for faster training
choices.rationale: This confuses a software support license with a hardware performance tier. AI Enterprise does not change GPU clocks; hardware performance is fixed by the GPU product itself, not by the subscription.

choices.choice_text: Bundled DGX Cloud compute credits for model training workloads
choices.rationale: This conflates a software license with a compute service. DGX Cloud capacity is purchased separately; an AI Enterprise subscription covers the software stack and its support, not cloud compute time.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An infrastructure team managing a multi-node DGX cluster needs to schedule distributed training jobs, enforce user quotas, and monitor GPU utilization across the cluster. Which NVIDIA offering is designed for this role?

choices.choice_text: NVIDIA Base Command
choices.rationale: Base Command is NVIDIA's platform for operating DGX infrastructure: it handles job scheduling, multi-node orchestration, user and quota management, and cluster-wide telemetry such as GPU utilization.

choices.choice_text: NGC Catalog
choices.rationale: This confuses the software registry with cluster operations. NGC distributes containers, models, and charts; it has no scheduler, quota system, or cluster monitoring capability.

choices.choice_text: Triton Inference Server
choices.rationale: This mixes up inference serving with cluster management. Triton serves trained models and manages inference requests on a node; it does not schedule training jobs or administer users across a cluster.

choices.choice_text: NeMo framework
choices.rationale: This confuses the training framework with the platform that runs it. NeMo defines and trains generative-AI models; the jobs it produces still need a platform like Base Command for scheduling and monitoring.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A two-person team must self-host a Llama model behind an OpenAI-compatible API in production within a week. They have GPUs but little TensorRT-LLM or Triton expertise. Which approach delivers an optimized endpoint fastest?

choices.choice_text: Deploy the model's prebuilt NIM microservice container
choices.rationale: NIM packages an optimized inference engine (built on TensorRT-LLM and Triton) with a standard OpenAI-compatible API in a self-hostable container, so the team gets production-grade performance without building the stack themselves.

choices.choice_text: Hand-build a TensorRT-LLM engine and a custom Triton backend
choices.rationale: This assumes maximum control is the fastest path. Building and tuning engines and backends by hand offers flexibility but demands exactly the expertise and time this team lacks.

choices.choice_text: Wrap Hugging Face generate() calls in a basic Flask server
choices.rationale: This reflects the belief that the simplest wrapper is production-ready. It works for demos, but it lacks optimized batching, an efficient inference engine, and the throughput needed for a production endpoint.

choices.choice_text: Call NVIDIA-hosted API catalog endpoints from the application
choices.rationale: This confuses prototyping against NVIDIA's hosted endpoints with self-hosting. The hosted API catalog runs on NVIDIA infrastructure, so it fails the requirement to serve the model on the team's own GPUs.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team building a RAG chatbot needs one NVIDIA component to embed and retrieve enterprise documents, and another to enforce conversation-safety policies at runtime. Which two components fit these roles? (Choose two.)

choices.choice_text: NeMo Retriever
choices.rationale: NeMo Retriever provides microservices for extracting, embedding, and reranking enterprise documents, covering the retrieval side of the RAG pipeline.

choices.choice_text: NeMo Guardrails
choices.rationale: NeMo Guardrails is a toolkit for adding programmable rails that constrain chatbot inputs, outputs, and dialog flow at runtime, covering the conversation-safety requirement.

choices.choice_text: NeMo Aligner
choices.rationale: The name suggests runtime safety, but Aligner is a training-time toolkit (RLHF, DPO, SteerLM) that changes model weights during alignment; it does not enforce policies on live conversations.

choices.choice_text: NeMo Curator
choices.rationale: Curator sounds like it could organize documents, but it cleans and deduplicates large corpora to prepare training data; it does not index or retrieve documents at query time.

choices.choice_text: TensorRT-LLM
choices.rationale: TensorRT-LLM optimizes and accelerates model execution during inference; it improves speed and efficiency but plays no role in document retrieval or safety policy enforcement.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team scales LLM training from one GPU to eight GPUs on a single node using PyTorch DistributedDataParallel, which relies on NCCL. What functionality does NCCL provide in this stack?

choices.choice_text: GPU-to-GPU collective communication primitives such as AllReduce and broadcast
choices.rationale: NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library) implements topology-aware collective operations (AllReduce, broadcast, all-gather, reduce-scatter) between GPUs, which frameworks like PyTorch DDP call to synchronize training across devices.

choices.choice_text: A compiler layer that translates model graphs into fused, optimized GPU kernels
choices.rationale: Kernel compilation and fusion is the role of tools like TensorRT or torch.compile, not NCCL. Candidates often lump all NVIDIA libraries together, but NCCL handles communication, not computation.

choices.choice_text: A cluster scheduler that assigns training jobs to available GPUs
choices.rationale: Job scheduling and GPU allocation are handled by orchestration systems such as Slurm or Kubernetes. NCCL operates inside an already-launched job to move data between GPUs, not to place jobs on them.

choices.choice_text: A memory manager that pools the memory of all eight GPUs into one address space
choices.rationale: NCCL does not aggregate or virtualize GPU memory; each GPU keeps its own memory. This confuses collective communication with unified/pooled memory features, which NCCL does not provide.

mcqQuestions.question_text: In data-parallel LLM training, each GPU processes a different mini-batch, then an AllReduce runs before the optimizer step. What does the AllReduce synchronize across GPUs?

choices.choice_text: Gradients, which are averaged so every replica applies the same update
choices.rationale: Each replica computes gradients on its own shard of data; AllReduce sums or averages these gradients so all replicas perform an identical optimizer step and their weights stay in sync.

choices.choice_text: Mini-batches of training data, so every GPU sees the same examples
choices.rationale: Data parallelism deliberately gives each GPU different examples to increase effective batch size and throughput. Sharing identical batches would defeat the purpose, and AllReduce never exchanges raw data.

choices.choice_text: Forward-pass activations, so all replicas compute an identical loss value
choices.rationale: Exchanging activations between devices is characteristic of tensor or pipeline parallelism, not data parallelism. In data parallelism each replica computes its own local loss independently.

choices.choice_text: Model weights, which are re-broadcast from GPU 0 after every step
choices.rationale: This describes a parameter-server or per-step broadcast pattern. In DDP-style training, weights start identical and remain identical because each replica applies the same averaged gradients, so no per-step weight broadcast is needed.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer asks why NCCL uses a ring-based AllReduce instead of having every GPU send its full gradient tensor to one GPU that aggregates and returns the result. What is the main advantage of the ring approach?

choices.choice_text: Data moves in chunks between neighboring GPUs, so no single device becomes a bandwidth bottleneck
choices.rationale: In Ring-AllReduce each GPU only exchanges gradient chunks with its two neighbors, spreading traffic evenly; per-GPU communication volume stays nearly constant as more GPUs are added, unlike a central aggregator whose link saturates.

choices.choice_text: Gradients are automatically compressed to lower precision before transmission, shrinking traffic
choices.rationale: The ring algorithm changes the communication pattern, not the data representation. Gradient compression or reduced-precision communication is a separate technique, not what makes the ring topology efficient.

choices.choice_text: GPUs no longer need to synchronize, so each replica can update its weights asynchronously
choices.rationale: Ring-AllReduce is still a synchronous collective: every GPU must participate and finishes with the same reduced result. It optimizes how synchronization happens, it does not eliminate it.

choices.choice_text: Each GPU runs backpropagation on only a fraction of the model, cutting per-device compute load
choices.rationale: Every replica still runs full backpropagation and computes the complete gradient for its own mini-batch. The ring only changes how those gradients are communicated and reduced afterward, not how much backward-pass computation each GPU performs.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A 7B-parameter model fits comfortably in one GPU's memory, but training on a single GPU is too slow. The team adds seven more identical GPUs. Which distributed strategy most directly speeds up training here?

choices.choice_text: Data parallelism: replicate the model on every GPU, each processing a different data shard
choices.rationale: Since the full model fits on one GPU, replicating it and splitting each global batch across the eight GPUs multiplies throughput with only gradient synchronization as overhead — the standard first choice when memory is not the constraint.

choices.choice_text: Tensor parallelism: split each layer's weight matrices across the eight GPUs
choices.rationale: Tensor parallelism is motivated by models too large for one device; it adds frequent inter-GPU activation communication within layers. Applying it when the model already fits introduces overhead without necessity.

choices.choice_text: Pipeline parallelism: place different layers on different GPUs and pass activations along
choices.rationale: Pipeline parallelism also exists to fit a large model across devices and introduces pipeline 'bubbles' that idle GPUs. It is not the most direct way to accelerate a model that fits on a single GPU.

choices.choice_text: CPU offloading: move optimizer states to host memory to free GPU memory
choices.rationale: Offloading trades speed for memory savings by shuttling data over the comparatively slow CPU-GPU link. It addresses memory pressure, which is not the problem here, and typically slows each step down.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A product manager asks why LLM training runs on GPUs rather than general-purpose CPUs. Which characteristic of deep learning workloads best explains the fit?

choices.choice_text: Training is dominated by large matrix multiplications whose many independent operations map onto thousands of parallel cores
choices.rationale: Deep learning training consists overwhelmingly of dense matrix multiplications and similar tensor operations made of vast numbers of independent multiply-accumulates. GPUs devote their silicon to thousands of parallel arithmetic cores (plus tensor cores), so this regular, data-parallel math achieves far higher throughput than on a CPU's few latency-optimized cores.

choices.choice_text: GPU cores run at much higher clock speeds than CPU cores, so each individual operation finishes sooner
choices.rationale: GPU cores typically run at lower clock speeds than CPU cores; the advantage comes from executing many operations simultaneously, not from finishing any single operation faster.

choices.choice_text: GPUs offer more total memory than CPU-based systems, letting entire large models stay resident on-device
choices.rationale: GPU memory (tens of GB of HBM) is usually smaller than available system RAM; large models often must be sharded across GPUs. Memory capacity is a constraint of GPUs, not the reason they suit deep learning.

choices.choice_text: Deep learning consists mostly of sequential, branch-heavy control logic that GPU cores execute efficiently
choices.rationale: Sequential, branch-heavy control flow is exactly where CPUs excel and GPUs struggle. Deep learning is instead dominated by regular, data-parallel arithmetic, which is why it maps well to GPUs.

mcqQuestions.objective_title: Build LLM apps with Python libraries
mcqQuestions.question_text: A developer loads a pretrained checkpoint with AutoModelForCausalLM but instantiates a tokenizer from a different model family. The script runs without errors, yet every generation is incoherent. What best explains why the tokenizer must match the checkpoint?

choices.choice_text: Mismatched tokenizers differ only in special tokens such as BOS and EOS, so inserting the right control tokens manually would restore coherent output.
choices.rationale: Vocabularies differ far beyond special tokens: merge rules and ID assignments for ordinary subwords vary across tokenizers. Fixing BOS/EOS alone cannot realign the text-to-ID mapping with the model's embedding matrix.

choices.choice_text: The tokenizer stores part of the model's weights, so a true mismatch would raise a shape error when the checkpoint loads.
choices.rationale: Tokenizers hold vocabulary and merge rules, not model weights, so the checkpoint loads independently of which tokenizer is instantiated. As long as the mismatched IDs stay within the embedding matrix's range, nothing errors; the problem surfaces only as degraded generation quality.

choices.choice_text: Token IDs index the model's learned embedding matrix, so a different vocabulary feeds the model IDs that map to unrelated tokens.
choices.rationale: Correct. During pretraining, each embedding row and output logit is tied to a specific token ID in the checkpoint's vocabulary. A mismatched tokenizer assigns different IDs to the same text, so the model looks up embeddings for tokens the user never wrote, producing incoherent output.

choices.choice_text: Tokenizers differ mainly in encoding speed, so incoherent output must instead come from poorly chosen generation parameters.
choices.rationale: Fast versus slow tokenizer implementations affect throughput, not the mapping from text to IDs. A mismatched vocabulary corrupts the model's input regardless of temperature or other generation settings.

mcqQuestions.objective_title: Build LLM apps with Python libraries
mcqQuestions.question_text: A team is building a document Q&A assistant. One workstream needs connectors to ingest, chunk, and index a large corpus for retrieval; another needs to orchestrate multi-step chains combining prompts, tools, and conversation memory. Which pairing matches each framework's primary design focus?

choices.choice_text: LlamaIndex for corpus ingestion and indexing; LangChain for orchestrating the multi-step chains and tools.
choices.rationale: Correct. LlamaIndex centers on data connectors, chunking, and index/query abstractions for retrieval over private data, while LangChain centers on composing chains, agents, tools, and memory. The frameworks overlap, but this pairing reflects each one's primary focus.

choices.choice_text: LangChain for corpus ingestion and indexing; LlamaIndex for orchestrating the multi-step chains and tools.
choices.rationale: This reverses the specialties: LangChain's core abstractions are chains, agents, and memory, while LlamaIndex's are data loaders and indices. Swapping them misattributes what each framework was built around.

choices.choice_text: LangChain for both tasks, because LlamaIndex is a vector database rather than an application framework.
choices.rationale: LlamaIndex is an application framework that connects to vector stores such as FAISS or Milvus; it is not itself a vector database. This confuses the indexing framework with the storage backend it uses.

choices.choice_text: LlamaIndex for both tasks, because LangChain is limited to formatting prompt templates for single calls.
choices.rationale: LangChain goes well beyond prompt templating: it provides chains, agent executors, tool integrations, and memory, which is exactly the orchestration workstream described in the scenario.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer adds Milvus to a retrieval-augmented generation app that already includes an embedding model and an LLM. At query time, what is the vector database's role?

choices.choice_text: It converts the user's query and the documents into embeddings before any search can happen.
choices.rationale: Producing embeddings is the embedding model's job. The vector database stores and searches vectors it is given; it does not encode text itself.

choices.choice_text: It stores the LLM's weights in compressed form so the relevant parameters can be paged in per query.
choices.rationale: Vector databases hold document vectors and associated metadata, not model parameters. Model weights live in the inference runtime, and retrieval does not select among them.

choices.choice_text: It caches previous model completions so repeated prompts can skip calling the LLM.
choices.rationale: That describes a semantic or response cache. In a RAG pipeline the vector database retrieves source chunks to ground new generations, rather than replaying saved answers.

choices.choice_text: It performs similarity search over stored document embeddings, returning the chunks nearest to the query embedding.
choices.rationale: Correct. The vector database holds precomputed document embeddings and, given the embedded query, runs approximate nearest-neighbor search to surface the most semantically similar chunks, which are then added to the LLM's prompt context.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A developer registers a get_weather function schema with an LLM that supports tool calling. Mid-conversation, the model decides the function is needed. What happens next in the control flow?

choices.choice_text: The model executes get_weather directly inside the inference runtime and inserts the live API result into its reply.
choices.rationale: The model only generates tokens; it has no ability to execute code or reach external APIs from within inference. Execution responsibility belongs to the application.

choices.choice_text: The model emits the function name and arguments as structured output; the application runs the call and returns the result.
choices.rationale: Correct. In tool calling the model proposes a call as a structured payload (function name plus JSON arguments). The application code executes it and passes the result back as a tool message, and the model then continues generating with that information.

choices.choice_text: The hosted serving provider executes the function on its own backend infrastructure, so the developer only needs to register the schema.
choices.rationale: For developer-defined functions, the schema only tells the model what calls it may propose; the provider does not run the developer's code. The client application must execute the call and return the output.

choices.choice_text: The model predicts what get_weather would return based on its training data, so no external code needs to run.
choices.rationale: If no code runs, any 'result' the model writes is a hallucination. Tool calling exists precisely to inject real, current data by having the application execute the call and feed back the observation.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Reviewing an agent's trace, a developer sees repeating Thought, Action, and Observation entries before a final answer. In the ReAct prompting pattern, what is being interleaved?

choices.choice_text: Outputs from two separate models, one specialized for planning and another specialized for executing tool calls.
choices.rationale: ReAct is a prompting pattern for a single model: the same model produces both the reasoning text and the action selections within one generation loop. No second planner or executor model is required.

choices.choice_text: Fine-tuning updates with inference passes, so the agent's weights learn from each new observation it records.
choices.rationale: No weights change during a ReAct loop. The agent adapts in-context as observations accumulate in the prompt, which is distinct from fine-tuning.

choices.choice_text: Natural-language reasoning steps with tool-invoking actions, where each returned observation informs the next reasoning step.
choices.rationale: Correct. ReAct alternates generated reasoning traces (thoughts) with actions such as tool or API calls; each action's observation is appended to the context and conditions the next thought, continuing until the model emits a final answer.

choices.choice_text: Retrieval queries with reranking passes over a vector index until a top-ranked document contains the answer.
choices.rationale: That describes a retrieval-and-rerank pipeline in RAG. A ReAct action may include search, but the pattern's defining feature is interleaving reasoning with arbitrary actions and their observations, not repeated reranking.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An LLM API served with static batching shows GPU utilization dropping mid-batch: short completions finish early, but their slots stay idle until the longest request completes. Which serving technique most directly fixes this?

choices.choice_text: In-flight batching, which adds and removes requests at each decoding iteration
choices.rationale: In-flight (continuous) batching schedules at the iteration level: finished sequences free their slots immediately and queued requests join mid-batch, keeping the GPU busy instead of waiting for the whole batch to drain.

choices.choice_text: Increasing the static batch size so that many more requests are grouped into every forward pass
choices.rationale: A larger static batch worsens the problem: slots freed by early-finishing requests still sit idle until the entire, now larger, batch completes. The head-of-line blocking is inherent to static scheduling, not batch size.

choices.choice_text: Lowering the sampling temperature so completions terminate in fewer decoding steps
choices.rationale: Temperature shapes the randomness of token sampling, not generation length in any reliable way. Completion lengths would still vary widely across the batch, leaving the idle-slot problem intact.

choices.choice_text: Applying INT8 weight quantization to shorten the time spent in every forward pass
choices.rationale: Quantization speeds up each step and reduces memory, but it does nothing about slots left empty by early-finishing sequences; the utilization loss comes from the scheduling policy, not per-step compute.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team wants lower per-token latency for a large model while keeping its output distribution unchanged, so they enable speculative decoding. Which mechanism does this technique rely on?

choices.choice_text: A small draft model proposes several tokens that the target model verifies in one pass
choices.rationale: The cheap draft model autoregressively proposes a short run of tokens, and the large target model scores them all in a single parallel forward pass, accepting those consistent with its own distribution. The verification step is why output quality matches decoding with the target model alone.

choices.choice_text: The target model skips some transformer layers whenever the next token is easy to predict
choices.rationale: That describes early-exit or adaptive-depth inference, a different optimization. Speculative decoding always runs the full target model, using it to verify the draft's proposals.

choices.choice_text: Two models of similar size generate independently and their output logits are averaged
choices.rationale: Averaging logits is an ensemble technique aimed at accuracy; it increases compute per token and changes the output distribution rather than reducing latency.

choices.choice_text: Draft-model tokens are accepted directly whenever its confidence exceeds a probability threshold
choices.rationale: Accepting draft tokens without target-model verification would let the weaker model's errors through and change the output distribution. The acceptance test against the target model is exactly what speculative decoding adds.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A 70B-parameter model must be served on a single 48 GB GPU. FP16 weights need about 140 GB and INT8 about 70 GB. Which quantization approach fits the memory budget while keeping the model usable?

choices.choice_text: Use 4-bit weight quantization such as INT4, accepting a modest quality reduction
choices.rationale: At 4 bits per weight the model needs roughly 35 GB, leaving headroom for the KV cache within 48 GB. Modern 4-bit methods (e.g., AWQ, GPTQ) typically keep the accuracy loss modest.

choices.choice_text: Use FP8 quantization, since halving the FP16 memory brings the weights within budget
choices.rationale: FP8 stores about 1 byte per weight, roughly 70 GB for this model, which still exceeds the 48 GB card. Halving FP16 is not enough here.

choices.choice_text: Quantize only the KV cache while keeping the FP16 weights for full output quality
choices.rationale: The FP16 weights alone are about 140 GB, so shrinking only the cache cannot make the model fit. KV-cache quantization helps once the weights already fit.

choices.choice_text: Use INT8 weights and reduce the maximum batch size to fit the remaining memory
choices.rationale: INT8 weights are about 70 GB before any KV cache is allocated. Batch size affects cache and activation memory, not the weight footprint, so no batch setting makes INT8 fit on 48 GB.

mcqQuestions.objective_title: Software engineering best practices for LLM systems
mcqQuestions.question_text: Weeks after launch, users increasingly report low-quality answers from an LLM application, even though the model, prompts, and offline benchmark scores are unchanged since release. Which monitoring practice would most directly surface the cause?

choices.choice_text: Monitor production input distributions and run scheduled evals on sampled live traffic
choices.rationale: With the system unchanged, the likely cause is input drift: real user queries have shifted away from what the app was validated on. Comparing live traffic distributions and evaluating sampled production queries detects this divergence directly.

choices.choice_text: Re-run the original offline benchmark suite on a much more frequent release schedule
choices.rationale: The benchmark is a fixed dataset, so however often it runs it can only re-measure the same release-time queries. It carries no information about how production traffic has changed since launch.
