choices.choice_text: Add dashboards tracking per-request cost and end-to-end latency percentiles
choices.rationale: Cost and latency dashboards catch performance and spend regressions, which are a different failure mode. They carry no signal about answer quality degrading as inputs shift.

choices.choice_text: Increase logging verbosity on the inference servers to capture detailed request metadata
choices.rationale: Verbose infrastructure logs record what happened operationally but include no quality evaluation or distribution comparison, so drift in user queries would remain invisible.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer is sizing a GPU to serve a 7B-parameter model in FP16 for many concurrent long-context chat sessions. What is the approximate weight memory, and which additional memory component scales with concurrency and sequence length?

choices.choice_text: About 14 GB for weights, plus a per-request KV cache that grows with context
choices.rationale: FP16 stores 2 bytes per parameter, so 7B parameters is roughly 14 GB. Each active request also stores keys and values for every context and generated token, so KV memory grows with both batch size and sequence length.

choices.choice_text: About 28 GB for weights, plus activation memory that dominates GPU usage at inference
choices.rationale: 4 bytes per parameter is FP32, not FP16. Large activation memory dominating is characteristic of training; during incremental decoding, the KV cache is the component that grows.

choices.choice_text: About 7 GB for weights, plus optimizer state that is allocated for each active request
choices.rationale: 1 byte per parameter corresponds to INT8 or FP8, not FP16. Optimizer states (e.g., Adam moments) exist only during training and are never allocated per inference request.

choices.choice_text: About 14 GB for weights, with per-request memory staying fixed regardless of context length
choices.rationale: The weight arithmetic is right, but the KV cache grows roughly linearly with tokens processed, so long contexts and many concurrent sessions can exhaust VRAM even when the weights fit comfortably.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Before shipping an LLM to a memory-constrained edge device, a team must reduce the model's actual size, not just change how it is served. Which two techniques reduce the model itself? (Choose two.)

choices.choice_text: Knowledge distillation into a smaller student model
choices.rationale: Distillation trains a compact student to imitate the larger teacher's outputs, producing a new model with fewer parameters — a genuinely smaller artifact to deploy.

choices.choice_text: Structured pruning of redundant weights and attention heads
choices.rationale: Pruning removes low-importance weights, heads, or layers from the network itself, directly cutting parameter count and on-device footprint.

choices.choice_text: Continuous batching of incoming requests in the inference server
choices.rationale: Continuous batching is a scheduler-level serving optimization that improves throughput. The model's parameters and file size are completely untouched.

choices.choice_text: Speculative decoding with an auxiliary draft model
choices.rationale: Speculative decoding reduces latency at serving time and actually requires deploying an extra draft model alongside the target, so total deployed size goes up, not down.

choices.choice_text: Paged management of the KV cache during generation
choices.rationale: Paged KV-cache management (as in PagedAttention) reduces runtime memory fragmentation for requests in flight; it changes serving behavior but does not alter the model's parameters.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Your team fine-tuned a model for English-to-German translation and holds professionally produced reference translations for a held-out test set. You need a widely reported automatic metric to compare candidate checkpoints. Which metric is the standard choice?

choices.choice_text: BLEU, which computes modified n-gram precision of model outputs against the references
choices.rationale: BLEU is the long-established automatic metric for machine translation: it measures modified n-gram precision against one or more references and applies a brevity penalty, making it the conventional checkpoint-comparison score when references exist.

choices.choice_text: ROUGE-L, which measures recall of the longest common subsequence shared with the references
choices.rationale: Candidates often treat overlap metrics as interchangeable, but ROUGE is recall-oriented and is the convention for summarization, not translation; BLEU is the standard reported metric for MT.

choices.choice_text: Perplexity, computed by scoring the reference translations under the fine-tuned model
choices.rationale: Perplexity measures how well the model predicts text intrinsically; it never compares the model's generated translations to the references, so it does not assess translation output quality.

choices.choice_text: Exact-match accuracy between each generated sentence and its reference translation
choices.rationale: This assumes one correct output per input, but translation admits many valid phrasings; exact match would score most acceptable translations as failures, which is why partial n-gram overlap metrics like BLEU are used.

mcqQuestions.question_text: In a metrics review, a teammate asks why BLEU is conventional for machine translation while ROUGE is conventional for summarization. Which statement best captures the core difference in how the two metrics are oriented?

choices.choice_text: BLEU emphasizes n-gram precision of the candidate with a brevity penalty, while ROUGE emphasizes recall of the reference content
choices.rationale: BLEU asks how much of the candidate's n-grams appear in the references (precision), penalizing overly short outputs with a brevity penalty; ROUGE asks how much of the reference is captured by the candidate (recall), which suits checking that a summary covers key content.

choices.choice_text: BLEU emphasizes recall of the reference n-grams, while ROUGE emphasizes precision of the candidate and applies a brevity penalty to short outputs
choices.rationale: This reverses the two metrics' orientations; the brevity penalty belongs to BLEU (a precision metric needs it to discourage very short outputs), and recall orientation belongs to ROUGE.

choices.choice_text: BLEU compares contextual embeddings of the texts, while ROUGE counts surface n-gram overlap
choices.rationale: Both BLEU and ROUGE are surface n-gram (or subsequence) overlap metrics; embedding-based comparison describes metrics like BERTScore, not BLEU.

choices.choice_text: BLEU can be computed without any references, while ROUGE requires at least one human-written reference
choices.rationale: Both metrics are reference-based and cannot be computed without references; reference-free evaluation is the domain of intrinsic metrics like perplexity or of learned judges.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A chatbot produces open-ended, creative responses. BLEU against a single reference correlates poorly with human ratings because many differently worded responses are acceptable. Which evaluation approach better fits this task?

choices.choice_text: Score responses with an LLM judge or an embedding-based metric such as BERTScore
choices.rationale: LLM-as-a-judge and embedding-based metrics like BERTScore assess semantic quality rather than exact wording, so they credit valid paraphrases and typically correlate better with human judgments on open-ended generation.

choices.choice_text: Raise the BLEU n-gram order from four to six to capture longer phrase matches
choices.rationale: This assumes the problem is match granularity, but longer n-grams make surface matching even stricter; the real issue is that BLEU cannot recognize semantically equivalent wording at any n-gram order.

choices.choice_text: Switch from BLEU to ROUGE-1 so recall-oriented overlap credits paraphrased wording
choices.rationale: ROUGE still counts surface token overlap; changing the orientation from precision to recall does not let the metric recognize paraphrases that use different words.

choices.choice_text: Report the model's perplexity on the reference responses as the quality score
choices.rationale: Perplexity measures how well the model predicts text, not whether its generated responses are helpful, relevant, or high quality; a fluent model can score low perplexity while producing poor answers.

mcqQuestions.question_text: You benchmark a code-generation model by sampling several candidate programs per problem and executing each against hidden unit tests. You want a standard metric that reflects functional correctness rather than textual similarity to reference solutions. Which metric should you report?

choices.choice_text: pass@k, the probability that at least one of k sampled programs passes the tests
choices.rationale: pass@k is the standard code-generation metric (used in HumanEval-style benchmarks): it estimates the probability that at least one of k sampled programs passes all unit tests, directly measuring functional correctness.

choices.choice_text: CodeBLEU, which augments n-gram overlap with syntax and data-flow matching against reference solutions
choices.rationale: CodeBLEU is still a similarity metric against a reference implementation; a program can score high on similarity yet fail the tests, or pass the tests while looking nothing like the reference.

choices.choice_text: Exact-match accuracy between each generated program and the reference implementation
choices.rationale: This assumes one canonical solution, but many textually different programs are functionally correct; exact match would mark most working solutions as wrong.

choices.choice_text: ROUGE-L computed over the token sequences of generated and reference programs
choices.rationale: ROUGE-L measures subsequence overlap for natural-language summarization; applied to code it captures surface similarity only and says nothing about whether the program compiles or passes tests.

mcqQuestions.question_text: After continued pre-training on legal text, a researcher reports that the model's held-out perplexity on legal documents fell from 12 to 8. Which statement correctly interprets this result?

choices.choice_text: The model now assigns higher probability to unseen domain text; this intrinsic gain does not directly measure downstream task performance
choices.rationale: Perplexity is an intrinsic language-modeling metric: lower values mean the model predicts held-out text with higher probability. It requires no reference answers and, while often correlated with capability, it is not itself a downstream task score.

choices.choice_text: The result is invalid because perplexity cannot be computed without reference answers paired with each document
choices.rationale: This confuses perplexity with reference-based metrics like BLEU or ROUGE; perplexity only needs the held-out text itself, since the model is scored on predicting each next token.

choices.choice_text: The drop indicates degraded modeling, since higher perplexity reflects a more confident language model
choices.rationale: This inverts the metric's direction: perplexity is the exponentiated average negative log-likelihood, so lower perplexity means higher probability assigned to the text, i.e., better prediction, not worse.

choices.choice_text: The drop confirms proportionally higher accuracy on legal question-answering benchmarks, since perplexity directly measures task quality
choices.rationale: Perplexity often correlates with downstream capability but is not a task score; a model can predict legal prose well yet still answer legal QA questions poorly, so benchmark accuracy must be measured separately.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A fraud-detection classifier is evaluated on a dataset where 1% of transactions are fraudulent. The model reports 99% accuracy, yet in production it catches almost no fraud. What is the most likely explanation the team should investigate first?

choices.choice_text: It is labeling nearly every transaction as legitimate, so accuracy simply mirrors the 99:1 class ratio.
choices.rationale: With 99% negatives, a model that predicts the majority class for everything scores 99% accuracy while detecting zero fraud. Accuracy is uninformative under heavy class imbalance; a confusion matrix or minority-class recall exposes the failure.

choices.choice_text: It has memorized the training data, since near-perfect accuracy is a classic sign of overfitting to seen examples.
choices.rationale: Overfitting shows up as a gap between training and held-out performance, not as high held-out accuracy by itself. Here 99% accuracy is achievable with no learning at all, so imbalance is the more direct explanation.

choices.choice_text: There is leakage between the training and test splits, which is the usual cause of accuracy above 95 percent.
choices.rationale: Leakage inflates test scores by exposing test information during training, but it is not implied by a high score alone. On a 99:1 dataset, 99% accuracy requires no leakage — the majority-class baseline already achieves it.

choices.choice_text: The test set is unrepresentative, and rebalancing it to a 50:50 split would reveal the model's true accuracy.
choices.rationale: The test set should reflect the real deployment distribution, and 1% fraud may well be realistic. The problem is the choice of metric, not the split; artificially rebalancing the test set would distort estimates of production behavior.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A medical team deploys a classifier that flags radiology reports for possible tumors so a radiologist can review them. Missing a real tumor is far more harmful than reviewing a benign report. Which metric should the team prioritize when tuning the model?

choices.choice_text: Recall, because it measures the fraction of actual tumor cases the model successfully flags.
choices.rationale: Recall (sensitivity) is true positives divided by all actual positives, so maximizing it directly minimizes missed tumors — the costly error in this scenario. Extra benign reviews are an acceptable trade-off.

choices.choice_text: Precision, because it keeps the review queue trustworthy by minimizing false alarms sent to radiologists.
choices.rationale: Precision measures how many flagged reports are truly positive, which controls reviewer workload, not missed cases. Prioritizing precision here would tolerate false negatives — exactly the error the scenario says is most harmful.

choices.choice_text: Accuracy, because overall correctness across both classes matters most in clinical settings.
choices.rationale: Accuracy weights both error types equally and is dominated by the majority (benign) class, so a model that misses many tumors can still score highly. It does not reflect the asymmetric cost structure described.

choices.choice_text: Specificity, because correctly clearing benign reports reduces unnecessary radiologist workload.
choices.rationale: Specificity measures how well negatives are identified, so optimizing it reduces false alarms, not missed tumors. Pushing specificity up typically trades away sensitivity, increasing the dangerous false-negative errors.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Two entity-extraction models are compared: Model A has precision 0.95 and recall 0.40; Model B has precision 0.78 and recall 0.74. The team wants one score that rewards balancing precision and recall rather than excelling at only one. Which summary metric fits best?

choices.choice_text: F1 score, because the harmonic mean is pulled toward the lower of precision and recall.
choices.rationale: The harmonic mean penalizes imbalance: Model A's F1 is about 0.56 despite 0.95 precision, while Model B's is about 0.76. A model cannot score well on F1 by sacrificing one component for the other.

choices.choice_text: The arithmetic mean of precision and recall, because it weighs both components of performance equally fairly.
choices.rationale: The arithmetic mean lets a very high value mask a very low one — Model A would average 0.675, understating how badly its 0.40 recall hurts extraction coverage. Equal weighting is not the same as rewarding balance.

choices.choice_text: Accuracy, because it condenses all four confusion-matrix cells into a single overall score.
choices.rationale: Accuracy is dominated by true negatives, which are ill-defined and enormous in extraction tasks (every non-entity span counts). It says nothing about the precision-recall balance the team cares about.

choices.choice_text: ROC-AUC, because it summarizes model quality across every possible decision threshold in one number.
choices.rationale: ROC-AUC measures ranking quality across all thresholds, not the precision-recall balance at the single operating point the team will deploy. It also presumes ranked scores over a well-defined set of negatives, which entity extraction does not naturally provide.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An anomaly detector is evaluated on traffic where 0.5% of events are true anomalies. It scores 0.96 ROC-AUC, yet analysts report that most flagged events are false alarms. Which evaluation view best explains this gap?

choices.choice_text: A precision-recall curve, because the ROC false-positive rate stays tiny when negatives vastly outnumber positives.
choices.rationale: With 200 negatives per positive, even a 2% false-positive rate produces several false alarms per true detection, so precision collapses while ROC-AUC stays high. The PR curve conditions on predicted positives and exposes this directly.

choices.choice_text: A calibration plot, because the frequent false alarms indicate that the model's predicted probabilities are poorly calibrated.
choices.rationale: Calibration concerns whether predicted probabilities match observed frequencies, not how many false alarms appear among flagged items. A perfectly calibrated model on 0.5% positives can still yield low precision at any usable threshold.

choices.choice_text: Overall accuracy at the deployed threshold, because AUC ignores where the operating threshold is actually set.
choices.rationale: It is true that AUC is threshold-free, but accuracy is also dominated by the 99.5% negative class and would look excellent despite the false-alarm problem. It cannot explain or reveal the precision failure.

choices.choice_text: A confusion matrix computed on a class-balanced resample, since rebalancing removes the imbalance from the evaluation.
choices.rationale: Rebalancing the evaluation set changes the prevalence and therefore the precision the analysts actually experience in production. Evaluation should reflect the deployment distribution; the fix is choosing prevalence-sensitive views like PR curves, not resampling.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Two regression models predict inference latency: one was trained with MSE loss (validation loss 14.2), the other with MAE loss (validation loss 2.3). A teammate declares the MAE model better because its loss is lower. What is the sound way to compare them?

choices.choice_text: Score both models on the same held-out set with a common metric such as explained variance (R²).
choices.rationale: Losses computed with different objective functions are on different scales (squared units versus absolute units), so their raw values are not comparable. A shared metric like R² on the same held-out data puts both models on one scale.

choices.choice_text: Accept the MAE model, since a lower validation loss directly indicates a better-fitting model.
choices.rationale: Lower loss only signals better fit when both models are measured with the same loss function on the same data. MSE squares errors while MAE does not, so 2.3 versus 14.2 reflects different units, not model quality.

choices.choice_text: Compare each model's percentage loss reduction from its initial value, since relative improvement is scale-free.
choices.rationale: Relative improvement measures how far training progressed from a starting point, not final predictive quality; a model can improve 90% from a terrible initialization and still be worse. It also remains confounded by the differing loss shapes.

choices.choice_text: Compare their training losses instead, since validation loss depends on the random split and is noisier.
choices.rationale: Training loss reflects fit to data the model has already seen and rewards overfitting, so it is the wrong basis for model selection. It also still suffers the same scale mismatch between MSE and MAE values.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team fine-tunes a BERT-style encoder for text classification and wants a standard benchmark to compare it against baseline models before deployment. A colleague suggests reporting GLUE scores. What does GLUE primarily evaluate?

choices.choice_text: Open-ended text generation quality measured against human-written reference summaries and translations
choices.rationale: This confuses GLUE with generation evaluation setups scored by metrics like ROUGE or BLEU. GLUE tasks are classification and regression over sentences, not free-form generation judged against references.

choices.choice_text: Factual world knowledge spanning dozens of academic and professional subjects
choices.rationale: This describes MMLU, which probes multi-subject knowledge with exam-style questions. GLUE instead tests linguistic understanding of sentences, not breadth of world knowledge.

choices.choice_text: Natural language understanding across tasks like entailment, sentiment, and similarity
choices.rationale: GLUE (General Language Understanding Evaluation) is a suite of sentence-level understanding tasks — including textual entailment, sentiment classification, and sentence similarity — designed to compare models on general NLU ability, which matches an encoder-classifier use case.

choices.choice_text: Translation accuracy across multiple language pairs and text domains
choices.rationale: Translation quality is evaluated with benchmarks like WMT and metrics like BLEU. GLUE is an English-language understanding suite and contains no translation tasks.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team evaluating candidate foundation models wants one benchmark that measures breadth of knowledge and reasoning using multiple-choice questions spanning dozens of subjects, from elementary math to law and medicine. Which benchmark should they report?

choices.choice_text: HELM
choices.rationale: HELM is broad, but it is a holistic evaluation framework that aggregates many scenarios and multiple metric axes (accuracy, robustness, fairness, efficiency) rather than a single multi-subject multiple-choice exam. Candidates often conflate the two because both are 'broad-capability' evaluations.

choices.choice_text: MMLU
choices.rationale: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) consists of multiple-choice questions across roughly 57 subjects, including STEM, law, and medicine, making it the standard single benchmark for broad knowledge and reasoning.

choices.choice_text: SQuAD
choices.rationale: SQuAD tests extractive reading comprehension — answering questions from a provided passage. It measures comprehension of given text, not breadth of subject-matter knowledge across disciplines.

choices.choice_text: GLUE
choices.rationale: GLUE covers sentence-level language understanding tasks such as entailment and similarity. It does not test multi-subject academic and professional knowledge with exam-style questions.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A new LLM posts a surprisingly high score on a public benchmark. An audit finds many of the benchmark's test questions and answers appeared verbatim in the model's pre-training corpus. How should the team interpret this score?

choices.choice_text: The score understates true ability because memorizing test items is a form of overfitting that degrades performance
choices.rationale: This misapplies the intuition that overfitting always hurts performance. Overfitting harms generalization to unseen data, but on the very items that were memorized, measured performance goes up — so contamination inflates the benchmark score rather than lowering it.

choices.choice_text: The score remains valid because pre-training exposure differs from fine-tuning directly on labeled test data
choices.rationale: This is a common rationalization, but leakage during any training phase compromises the test set. Verbatim exposure in pre-training lets the model memorize question-answer pairs just as fine-tuning would.

choices.choice_text: The score is unaffected because benchmark items are a negligible fraction of the pre-training corpus
choices.rationale: Corpus proportion is not what matters — large models can memorize sequences seen only a few times. Even a tiny contaminated fraction can materially inflate scores on exactly those test items.

choices.choice_text: The score is inflated because the model can recall memorized answers rather than generalize
choices.rationale: When test items leak into training data, the model can reproduce answers from memory, so the benchmark no longer measures generalization to unseen inputs. This is the core reason contamination invalidates a score.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A researcher evaluates an instruction-tuned LLM on a new classification task by giving only a task description in the prompt — no examples and no task-specific training. What does this zero-shot evaluation primarily measure?

choices.choice_text: How well the model generalizes to an unseen task from instructions alone
choices.rationale: Zero-shot evaluation withholds examples and task-specific training, so performance reflects the model's ability to apply pre-trained knowledge and instruction-following to a task it was never explicitly adapted for.

choices.choice_text: How effectively the model learns the task from labeled in-context examples supplied at inference
choices.rationale: Learning from in-context examples is few-shot evaluation. Zero-shot by definition provides no examples, so it cannot measure in-context learning from demonstrations.

choices.choice_text: The quality of the task-specific fine-tuning data used to adapt the model
choices.rationale: No task-specific fine-tuning occurs in a zero-shot setup, so there is no adaptation data to assess. This confuses zero-shot evaluation with evaluating a fine-tuned model.

choices.choice_text: The upper bound of accuracy achievable with fully optimized prompt engineering
choices.rationale: A zero-shot score with one plain task description is typically a baseline, not a ceiling — few-shot prompting or prompt optimization usually improves results. Treating it as an upper bound misreads what the setup controls for.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A RAG chatbot sometimes returns wrong answers. The team wants to determine whether failures originate in the retriever or in the generator before investing in fixes. Which evaluation approach best isolates the two failure sources?

choices.choice_text: Compute one end-to-end answer accuracy score, since a single number already reflects both components together
choices.rationale: An end-to-end score conflates the two stages: a wrong answer could stem from missing context or from the generator ignoring good context. It cannot tell the team which component to fix.

choices.choice_text: Evaluate only generator faithfulness, because a capable model compensates for weak retrieval
choices.rationale: A generator cannot reliably answer from context it never received; if retrieval misses the relevant passage, even a faithful generator will be wrong or forced to decline. Retrieval quality must be assessed on its own.

choices.choice_text: Score retrieval relevance and answer faithfulness to retrieved context as separate metrics
choices.rationale: RAG quality has two distinct axes: whether the retriever surfaces relevant passages (e.g., recall of gold documents) and whether the generator's answer is faithful to that retrieved context. Measuring each separately attributes failures to the right component.

choices.choice_text: Evaluate only retrieval precision, because relevant passages ensure the final answer is correct
choices.rationale: Retrieving the right passages does not ensure the answer is grounded in them — the generator can still contradict or ignore the context (hallucinate). Generation faithfulness must be measured as its own axis.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A chatbot team believes a rewritten system prompt will raise user thumbs-up rates in production. They want the most valid way to measure the prompt's effect. Which experiment design should they use?

choices.choice_text: Randomly assign each session 50/50 to the old or new prompt, keeping all other settings identical.
choices.rationale: Random assignment balances user and traffic characteristics across arms, and changing only the prompt isolates it as the single variable. Any difference in thumbs-up rate can then be attributed to the prompt change.

choices.choice_text: Run the old prompt for one week and the new prompt the following week, then compare the two weekly rates.
choices.rationale: Sequential (before/after) comparison confounds the prompt change with time-based effects such as traffic mix, seasonality, or news events. Candidates often treat this as an A/B test, but the groups are not concurrent or randomized.

choices.choice_text: Launch the new prompt together with an upgraded model version so the test shows the largest possible gain.
choices.rationale: Bundling two changes makes the result uninterpretable: any gain could come from the model upgrade rather than the prompt. Valid experiments vary one factor at a time per arm.

choices.choice_text: Invite users to opt in to the new prompt experience and compare their ratings against non-participants.
choices.rationale: Opt-in creates selection bias, since users who volunteer for new features differ systematically from those who do not. The comparison then reflects user differences, not the prompt's effect.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team has a promising fine-tuned candidate model and must decide how offline evaluation sets and online A/B tests fit into their release process. Which approach reflects the appropriate role of each?

choices.choice_text: Screen candidates with offline evaluation first, then confirm real user impact with an online A/B test.
choices.rationale: Offline evals are fast, cheap, and repeatable, making them ideal for filtering candidates before exposing users to changes. Online A/B tests then measure actual user-facing impact, which offline metrics only approximate.

choices.choice_text: Skip offline evaluation and A/B test every candidate directly, since production traffic is the only data that reflects users.
choices.rationale: A/B testing every candidate is slow, costly, and risks exposing users to regressions that offline checks would have caught. Online data is valuable, but it should validate pre-screened candidates, not replace screening.

choices.choice_text: Rely on offline benchmarks alone, since a model that wins offline will show the same gains with live users.
choices.rationale: Offline benchmarks often diverge from real user behavior because live traffic, interaction patterns, and preferences differ from curated test sets. This offline-online gap is exactly why A/B tests remain necessary before full rollout.

choices.choice_text: Reserve online A/B tests for interface changes, because model quality differences can only be detected offline.
choices.rationale: A/B tests apply to any user-visible change, including swapping models or prompts behind the same interface. Model quality differences are routinely measured online via engagement and satisfaction metrics.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer fine-tunes variants A and B once each; B scores 0.7 points higher on the held-out set. Before declaring B the winner, what should the engineer do?

choices.choice_text: Rerun both variants with several random seeds and compare the score distributions or confidence intervals.
choices.rationale: Fine-tuning runs vary with seed-dependent factors like initialization and data ordering, so a small single-run gap may be noise. Multiple seeds reveal whether B's advantage exceeds run-to-run variance.

choices.choice_text: Ship variant B now, because any positive difference on a held-out set reflects a genuine improvement.
choices.rationale: This assumes single-run scores are noise-free, but a 0.7-point gap can fall entirely within normal run-to-run variance. A held-out set controls overfitting, not stochastic training variability.

choices.choice_text: Re-run variant B once with the same random seed to confirm the identical score is reproduced.
choices.rationale: Reproducing the same seed checks determinism of the pipeline, not whether B is genuinely better than A. It provides no information about how scores vary across seeds.

choices.choice_text: Score both variants on the training data to verify the improvement holds there as well.
choices.rationale: Training-set scores mostly reflect memorization and cannot validate generalization. Agreement on training data says nothing about whether the held-out gap is statistically meaningful.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A data scientist has a small labeled dataset and worries a single train/test split gives an unreliable estimate of model quality. They choose 5-fold cross-validation. Which procedure is correct?

choices.choice_text: Partition the data into five folds, train on four and validate on the fifth, rotating until each fold is validated once, then average.
choices.rationale: This is the standard k-fold procedure: every example is used for validation exactly once and for training in the other rounds. Averaging the five scores yields a lower-variance estimate than one fixed split.

choices.choice_text: Train five models on the full dataset with five different random seeds and report the mean of their scores on that same data.
choices.rationale: This measures seed-to-seed variance while evaluating on training data, so it never tests generalization. Candidates often confuse multi-seed runs with cross-validation, but no data is held out here.

choices.choice_text: Fix one 80/20 split, train a single model, and evaluate it five separate times on the same held-out 20%.
choices.rationale: Evaluating the same trained model on the same test set merely repeats a single measurement and does nothing to reduce the variance introduced by the particular split. Cross-validation requires rotating which data is held out.

choices.choice_text: Resample the test portion with replacement five times and report the best of the five resulting scores.
choices.rationale: Taking the best of several resamples is cherry-picking and biases the estimate upward. Cross-validation averages across folds rather than selecting the most favorable result.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team must tune learning rate, LoRA rank, and dropout for a fine-tuning job under a fixed GPU-hour budget. Which statement about hyperparameter search strategies is accurate?

choices.choice_text: Random search tries more distinct values per hyperparameter than grid search at equal budget, while Bayesian optimization uses past trials to guide selection.
choices.rationale: A grid repeats the same few values per dimension, whereas random sampling covers many distinct values of the parameters that matter most. Bayesian optimization goes further by modeling past results to propose promising configurations, often reducing the trials needed.

choices.choice_text: Grid search makes the best use of a limited budget because exhaustively enumerating combinations ensures no region of the space is left unexplored.
choices.rationale: Grid search cost grows exponentially with the number of hyperparameters, so a fixed budget forces very coarse grids that leave large gaps. Exhaustiveness over a coarse grid is not the same as good coverage of the space.

choices.choice_text: Bayesian optimization selects each configuration independently of earlier results, so it behaves like random search with extra overhead.
choices.rationale: This confuses Bayesian optimization with random search: its defining feature is using a surrogate model of past trial outcomes to choose the next trial. That adaptivity is exactly what distinguishes it from independent sampling.

choices.choice_text: Random search cannot handle continuous hyperparameters such as learning rate or dropout, so grid search is required whenever continuous values must be tuned.
choices.rationale: Random search draws directly from continuous distributions, which is one of its strengths. It is grid search that must discretize continuous ranges into a fixed set of points.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A fine-tuned LLM posts unusually high scores on its held-out evaluation set, and reviewers suspect the eval data overlaps the training data. Which two practices help prevent this kind of leakage? (Choose two.)

choices.choice_text: Check for exact and near-duplicate overlap between the training corpus and the evaluation set, removing contaminated examples before training.
choices.rationale: Decontamination via exact and fuzzy deduplication directly removes eval examples the model would otherwise see during training. This is a standard defense against inflated benchmark scores.

choices.choice_text: Split at the source-document level so passages or paraphrases from one document never land in both training and evaluation sets.
choices.rationale: Splitting at the record level lets near-identical passages from the same source fall on both sides of the split. Grouping by source document before splitting keeps correlated content out of the eval set.

choices.choice_text: Reshuffle the combined training and evaluation pool between every epoch so that both sets draw evenly from the same underlying distribution.
choices.rationale: Reshuffling a combined pool moves examples across the train/eval boundary, which creates leakage rather than preventing it. The split must be fixed and disjoint for the evaluation to be valid.

choices.choice_text: Evaluate with a higher sampling temperature so the model is less likely to emit memorized training answers verbatim.
choices.rationale: Temperature changes decoding randomness but does not remove the model's exposure to eval content during training. Scores would still be inflated by contamination regardless of the sampling settings.

choices.choice_text: Fix the same random seed for both training and evaluation runs so results stay consistent across repetitions.
choices.rationale: Fixed seeds improve reproducibility of a given run but have no effect on whether eval examples appeared in the training data. Leakage is a data-partitioning problem, not a randomness problem.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer fine-tuning a 7B model with LoRA raises the adapter rank r from 8 to 64, keeping all other hyperparameters fixed. What is the primary trade-off introduced by the higher rank?

choices.choice_text: More adapter capacity to capture task-specific detail, at the cost of more trainable parameters and greater overfitting risk on small datasets.
choices.rationale: Rank r sets the dimensionality of the low-rank update matrices, so a higher r means more expressive adapters but also more trainable parameters, more adapter memory, and a higher chance of overfitting when the fine-tuning set is small.

choices.choice_text: Higher inference latency in production, because higher-rank adapters add extra layers that cannot be merged into the base model's weights at serving time.
choices.rationale: This reflects the misconception that LoRA permanently adds serving overhead. LoRA updates can be merged into the base weights after training, so rank has essentially no effect on inference latency in a merged deployment.

choices.choice_text: Stronger updates to the frozen base weights, which now require a proportionally lower learning rate to remain numerically stable.
choices.rationale: This confuses adapter capacity with base-model updates. In LoRA the base weights stay frozen regardless of rank; only the low-rank matrices are trained, and rank does not destabilize the frozen weights.

choices.choice_text: Lower overfitting risk, because the larger rank spreads the update across more dimensions and acts as a regularizer.
choices.rationale: This reverses the actual effect. A low rank is what constrains the update and acts as an implicit regularizer; raising the rank increases capacity and therefore tends to increase, not decrease, overfitting risk.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team fine-tunes a 13B model on a single 24 GB GPU using QLoRA. Which statement correctly describes where the 4-bit quantization is applied and why memory usage drops?

choices.choice_text: The frozen base model weights are stored in 4-bit precision, shrinking their footprint, while the small LoRA adapters train in higher precision.
choices.rationale: QLoRA quantizes the frozen base weights (typically to NF4), which are by far the largest memory consumer, while gradients flow through the quantized model into higher-precision LoRA adapters. This is why a 13B model can fit on one consumer-class GPU.

choices.choice_text: The LoRA adapter weights themselves are quantized to 4 bits during training, so gradient updates require a quarter of the usual memory.
choices.rationale: This misplaces the quantization. The adapters are the trainable parameters and remain in higher precision (e.g., BF16) so gradient descent works normally; they are tiny anyway, so quantizing them would save little memory.

choices.choice_text: The optimizer states and gradients for every base weight are quantized to 4 bits, which removes most of the Adam optimizer's memory overhead during training.
choices.rationale: This misses that the frozen base weights have no optimizer states or gradients at all — only the small adapter set does. QLoRA's 4-bit quantization targets the stored base weights, not per-weight optimizer states.

choices.choice_text: The activations and KV cache are compressed to 4 bits during forward passes, so longer sequences fit in memory during training.
choices.rationale: This confuses QLoRA with activation or KV-cache quantization techniques used at inference time. QLoRA's defining trick is 4-bit storage of the frozen base weights; activations are still computed in higher precision.

mcqQuestions.question_text: After full fine-tuning on a legal-contract corpus for several epochs, a model drafts contracts well but now fails general instructions it previously handled. What is the most likely cause, and which mitigation directly addresses it?

choices.choice_text: Catastrophic forgetting; mix general-domain instruction data into the fine-tuning batches so earlier capabilities are rehearsed.
choices.rationale: Losing previously learned general skills while gaining narrow-domain skill is the classic symptom of catastrophic forgetting, and replaying (mixing in) general data during fine-tuning directly counteracts it by keeping the old distribution in the training signal.

choices.choice_text: Underfitting of the legal domain; continue training for additional epochs on the legal corpus until performance on both task types recovers.
choices.rationale: The model already performs well on legal drafting, so it is not underfitting. More epochs on legal-only data would push the weights further from their general-purpose configuration and worsen the forgetting.

choices.choice_text: Data contamination; deduplicate the legal corpus against the evaluation set to restore valid scores on general benchmarks.
choices.rationale: Contamination inflates measured scores on overlapping test items; it does not cause a genuine loss of general capability. Deduplication improves evaluation validity but would not restore the degraded general behavior.

choices.choice_text: Vanishing gradients; increase the learning rate so weight updates propagate to the earlier transformer layers again.
choices.rationale: Vanishing gradients would show up as failure to learn the new task, not loss of old skills. Raising the learning rate makes weight drift larger and would typically amplify forgetting rather than fix it.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team is deciding between full fine-tuning and a parameter-efficient method such as LoRA for adapting a pretrained LLM. Which situation most strongly favors full fine-tuning?

choices.choice_text: A large in-domain corpus, a multi-GPU compute budget, and a target domain far from the model's pretraining distribution.
choices.rationale: Full fine-tuning pays off when there is enough data to update all weights without overfitting, enough compute to afford it, and a large domain shift that low-rank or prompt-level updates may not have the capacity to bridge.

choices.choice_text: A few thousand labeled examples and a single mid-range GPU, with the task close to the model's existing capabilities.
choices.rationale: This is the textbook PEFT scenario: small data risks overfitting a full update, a single GPU cannot hold full-model gradients and optimizer states for a large LLM, and a nearby task needs only a small adaptation.

choices.choice_text: A requirement to serve many customer-specific variants of one base model without duplicating its full weights for each.
choices.rationale: This favors adapters, not full fine-tuning. LoRA lets many lightweight adapters share one frozen base model, whereas full fine-tuning would produce a separate full-size checkpoint per customer.

choices.choice_text: A need to change only the tone and output format of responses on tasks the base model already performs well.
choices.rationale: Style and format adjustments are small deltas from existing behavior, well within the capacity of PEFT or even prompting. Spending full fine-tuning compute here adds cost and forgetting risk without a capability benefit.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A colleague asks how prompt tuning (a soft-prompt method like p-tuning) differs mechanically from LoRA when both adapt a frozen base model. Which statement is accurate?

choices.choice_text: Prompt tuning learns continuous virtual-token embeddings prepended to the input, while LoRA trains low-rank update matrices inside the model's weight layers.
choices.rationale: Prompt tuning optimizes soft-prompt vectors that act as extra input tokens and leaves every model weight untouched, whereas LoRA inserts trainable low-rank matrices alongside internal weight matrices such as the attention projections.

choices.choice_text: Prompt tuning automatically searches over discrete natural-language prompt wordings, while LoRA learns numeric parameters through gradient descent on the weights.
choices.rationale: This confuses prompt tuning with automated prompt engineering. Soft prompts are continuous embedding vectors trained by gradient descent, not searches over human-readable text; they generally do not correspond to real vocabulary words.

choices.choice_text: Prompt tuning updates the embedding table entries for existing vocabulary tokens, while LoRA retrains the full attention layers at reduced numerical precision.
choices.rationale: This is wrong on both sides: prompt tuning adds new virtual-token embeddings rather than editing the existing vocabulary table, and LoRA trains small low-rank additions, not full attention layers, with precision being a separate concern.

choices.choice_text: Both methods inject trainable matrices into attention projections, but prompt tuning uses a higher rank to compensate for fewer tuned layers.
choices.rationale: This treats prompt tuning as a variant of LoRA. Prompt tuning injects nothing into the weight layers at all; its trainable parameters live entirely at the input-embedding level, and it has no notion of rank inside attention projections.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer uses an LLM judge for pairwise comparisons between two chatbot variants and suspects the judge systematically favors whichever response appears first in the prompt. Which approach best detects and mitigates this position bias?

choices.choice_text: Raise the judge's sampling temperature so any preference for a particular position averages out across many evaluations.
choices.rationale: Higher temperature adds random noise to individual verdicts but does not remove a systematic positional preference; the bias remains in the expected verdict while variance simply increases. Randomness is not a substitute for a controlled design.

choices.choice_text: Run each pairwise comparison twice with the response order swapped, and count inconsistent verdicts as ties.
choices.rationale: Swapping order isolates position as the only changed variable: if the verdict flips with the swap, position bias is detected, and scoring inconsistent pairs as ties (or averaging both orders) removes the positional advantage from the final result.

choices.choice_text: Ask the judge to report a confidence score and discard any verdicts that fall below a chosen threshold.
choices.rationale: Confidence filtering assumes the judge can recognize its own bias, but position bias affects high-confidence verdicts too. Discarding low-confidence outputs neither reveals nor corrects a systematic preference for the first-listed response.

choices.choice_text: Switch to a judge model with a longer context window so both responses fit fully within the prompt.
choices.rationale: This confuses position bias with truncation. Position bias occurs even when both responses are fully visible in context; it stems from the judge's ordering preference, not from missing text.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team uses an LLM judge to score summaries from two models. Model A's summaries are consistently longer and receive higher judge scores, yet blinded human reviewers rate the two models as roughly equal in quality. Which judge limitation most likely explains the discrepancy?

choices.choice_text: Position bias — the judge favors whichever response it encounters first in the comparison prompt.
choices.rationale: Position bias depends on presentation order, not response length. The scenario's key signal is that higher scores track consistently longer outputs, which order-swapping would not explain.

choices.choice_text: Verbosity bias — the judge tends to rate longer, more detailed responses higher regardless of actual quality.
choices.rationale: LLM judges are known to over-reward length and apparent thoroughness. Since scores correlate with summary length while blinded humans see equal quality, verbosity bias is the most direct explanation.

choices.choice_text: Self-enhancement bias — the judge scores responses higher when they resemble its own writing style and typical outputs.
choices.rationale: Self-enhancement bias favors outputs stylistically similar to the judge model's own generations. Nothing in the scenario ties Model A's outputs to the judge's style; the observable pattern is a length effect.

choices.choice_text: Prompt sensitivity — small changes in the evaluation prompt's wording cause the judge's verdicts to flip.
choices.rationale: Prompt sensitivity produces unstable verdicts across prompt variations, not a consistent, directional preference for one model's longer outputs. The described gap is systematic, not erratic.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An LLM judge assigns inconsistent 1-10 quality scores to identical responses across runs, and its scoring standards drift between evaluation batches. Adding a rubric with per-criterion definitions and worked examples markedly improves consistency. Why does the rubric help?

choices.choice_text: It anchors scoring to explicit, observable criteria, reducing the judge's reliance on shifting internal standards.
choices.rationale: Without a rubric, the judge implicitly invents its own standards each time, which vary with sampling and context. Explicit criteria and examples constrain what each score level means, so repeated judgments converge on the same reference points.

choices.choice_text: It effectively lowers the judge model's sampling temperature, making its token-level outputs deterministic.
choices.rationale: A rubric is prompt content and does not alter decoding parameters; temperature is set independently. Consistency improves because the criteria are constrained, not because generation becomes deterministic.

choices.choice_text: It performs in-context fine-tuning of the judge, permanently updating its weights to encode the stated criteria.
choices.rationale: In-context learning conditions the model's outputs for the current prompt only; no weights are updated and nothing persists across calls. This confuses prompting with fine-tuning.

choices.choice_text: It lengthens the prompt, giving the model more computation per generated token to reason with.
choices.rationale: The benefit comes from the rubric's content, not its length; padding the prompt with irrelevant text would not improve scoring. Longer context is not inherently better reasoning.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A researcher fixes random seeds, hyperparameters, and data ordering, yet repeated fine-tuning runs on the same GPU yield slightly different metrics, and results shift further when a colleague reruns the script on another server. Which explanation best accounts for both observations?

choices.choice_text: The optimizer's momentum buffers accumulate randomness between steps that fixed seeds are unable to control on any hardware.
choices.rationale: Momentum is a deterministic function of the gradients it receives; it introduces no randomness of its own. This misattributes variance to the optimizer rather than to how the gradients are computed on the GPU.

choices.choice_text: Random seeds must be re-initialized before every training batch rather than once at the start of the script.
choices.rationale: Seeding once at startup is the standard, sufficient practice for controlling RNG-driven choices like initialization and shuffling. Per-batch reseeding addresses nothing here and would not explain cross-machine differences.

choices.choice_text: The tokenizer applies stochastic subword segmentation, producing different token sequences on every run of the pipeline.
choices.rationale: Standard tokenizers are deterministic: the same text yields the same token IDs on every run and every machine. Stochastic segmentation (e.g., BPE-dropout) is an opt-in training regularizer, not default behavior.

choices.choice_text: Nondeterministic GPU kernels, such as atomic floating-point reductions, combined with unpinned CUDA and library versions across environments.
choices.rationale: Some GPU kernels sum floating-point values in a nondeterministic order, so identical seeded runs can diverge slightly on the same machine. Different, unpinned framework and CUDA versions on the second server select different kernel implementations, explaining the larger cross-machine shift.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Before adopting 2,000 human-rated response-quality labels as ground truth for a benchmark, a team has two annotators independently label an overlapping subset and computes Cohen's kappa. What is the primary purpose of this step?

choices.choice_text: To check that the labels are consistent enough to serve as trustworthy ground truth, since low agreement signals ambiguous criteria.
choices.rationale: Inter-annotator agreement measures whether independent humans applying the same guidelines reach the same labels. Low kappa means the task or guidelines are too ambiguous or subjective, so the labels would be too noisy to validate models against.

choices.choice_text: To measure each individual annotator's accuracy by comparing their labels against verified correct answers already established for the subset.
choices.rationale: This confuses agreement with accuracy: no verified correct answers exist yet, which is exactly why agreement is measured instead. Kappa quantifies consistency between annotators, not correctness against a gold standard.

choices.choice_text: To detect and correct class imbalance in the distribution of quality ratings across the dataset.
choices.rationale: Kappa's adjustment for chance agreement is sometimes confused with imbalance handling, but it measures rater consistency, not the label distribution. Class balance is assessed with simple frequency counts, not agreement statistics.

choices.choice_text: To increase the effective dataset size by averaging the duplicate labels into a single consensus rating.
choices.rationale: Overlapping annotation actually spends budget re-labeling the same items, reducing unique coverage rather than expanding it. Consensus labels can be a byproduct, but computing kappa exists to quantify agreement, not to grow the dataset.

mcqQuestions.objective_title: Text data cleaning and feature engineering
mcqQuestions.question_text: A data engineer normalizes customer reviews. A Porter stemmer converts "studies" to "studi" and leaves "better" unchanged, while a POS-aware lemmatizer returns "study" and "good". What best explains the difference in outputs?

choices.choice_text: Stemming performs a dictionary lookup for each token, while lemmatization applies fixed suffix-stripping rules that occasionally truncate words incorrectly.
choices.rationale: This reverses the two techniques. Stemming is the rule-based suffix-stripping approach (which is why it produces truncated non-words like "studi"), while lemmatization is the method backed by a vocabulary lookup.

choices.choice_text: Lemmatization uses vocabulary and morphological analysis to return dictionary base forms; stemming applies heuristic suffix-stripping rules.
choices.rationale: Correct. A lemmatizer consults a lexicon and morphology (often with POS context) to map words to valid lemmas, handling irregular forms like "better" to "good", whereas a stemmer only chops affixes by rule and can emit non-words like "studi".

choices.choice_text: Lemmatization is stemming followed by a spell-correction pass that repairs truncated stems back into valid English words.
choices.rationale: Lemmatization is not a post-processing layer on top of stemming; it is an independent technique using morphological analysis and a lexicon. No spell-correction step is involved, and stemming could not recover "good" from "better" regardless.

choices.choice_text: The stemmer is misconfigured; with the correct language setting a stemmer produces the same dictionary lemmas as a lemmatizer.
choices.rationale: This reflects the misconception that the two techniques are interchangeable. Even a correctly configured stemmer produces truncated stems by design because it applies suffix rules without a vocabulary, so it cannot map irregular forms like "better" to "good".

mcqQuestions.question_text: A search team expands queries using labeled semantic relations, retrieving "domestic dog" as a synonym and "canine" as a hypernym of "dog". A teammate suggests word2vec could replace their WordNet lookups. What is the fundamental difference between these two resources?

choices.choice_text: WordNet is a human-curated lexical database of synsets with labeled relations; word2vec learns dense vectors from corpus co-occurrence.
choices.rationale: Correct. WordNet is a manually built lexical database organizing words into synonym sets (synsets) linked by explicit relations such as hypernymy, while word2vec is a neural method that learns unlabeled dense embeddings from co-occurrence patterns in text.

choices.choice_text: WordNet is a neural embedding model trained on encyclopedic text, while word2vec is a hand-built thesaurus of curated synonym lists.
choices.rationale: This reverses the two resources. WordNet is the hand-curated lexical database built by linguists, and word2vec is the model that learns vector representations from a training corpus.

choices.choice_text: Both are trained on corpora; WordNet differs only by using a larger context window that captures hypernym relations.
choices.rationale: WordNet is not trained on a corpus at all; its synsets and relations are manually curated. Context-window size is a word2vec hyperparameter and does not produce explicitly labeled hypernym links in either resource.

choices.choice_text: word2vec attaches relation labels such as synonym or hypernym to its nearest neighbors, while WordNet returns only unlabeled similarity scores.
choices.rationale: This is backwards. word2vec neighbors are ranked only by unlabeled cosine similarity, so a nearby vector could be a synonym, hypernym, or merely a related term; the explicitly labeled relations belong to WordNet.

mcqQuestions.objective_title: Text data cleaning and feature engineering
mcqQuestions.question_text: A media company must extract person and organization names plus part-of-speech tags from millions of news articles in a production pipeline, using pretrained components rather than training models from scratch. Which Python library best fits this job?

choices.choice_text: NLTK, whose comprehensive suite of taggers, chunkers, and corpora makes it the standard choice for large-scale production pipelines.
choices.rationale: NLTK is comprehensive and widely taught, but it is oriented toward education and research experimentation; its components are generally slower and not engineered for high-throughput production use. The misconception is that the most established toolkit is also the best production choice.

choices.choice_text: gensim, whose topic models surface named entities as the dominant topics within each article.
choices.rationale: gensim specializes in topic modeling and word embeddings (e.g., LDA, word2vec). Topic models group documents by themes; they do not perform token-level tasks like NER or POS tagging.

choices.choice_text: spaCy, which ships fast pretrained pipelines with NER and POS-tagging components.
choices.rationale: Correct. spaCy is built for production NLP and provides pretrained, efficient pipelines that include named-entity recognition and part-of-speech tagging out of the box, exactly matching this requirement.

choices.choice_text: scikit-learn, which includes pretrained sequence labelers for entity extraction in its text module.
choices.rationale: scikit-learn offers general-purpose ML algorithms and text vectorizers (e.g., TF-IDF) but ships no pretrained NER or POS models; the team would have to engineer features and train sequence models themselves.

mcqQuestions.question_text: While engineering features for document clustering, an analyst sees that "photosynthesis" receives a very high TF-IDF weight in one article from a mixed-topic news corpus. What does this high score indicate?

choices.choice_text: The term occurs often in that article but rarely across other documents, making it distinctive.
choices.rationale: Correct. TF-IDF multiplies term frequency within the document by inverse document frequency across the corpus, so a high value flags a term that is both locally frequent and globally rare, i.e., discriminative for that document.

choices.choice_text: The term is among the most frequent words across the entire corpus, so it carries the most information.
choices.rationale: This ignores the IDF component. Terms frequent across the whole corpus appear in many documents, which drives inverse document frequency down and yields low TF-IDF weights, not high ones.

choices.choice_text: The term is likely a stopword that the preprocessing step failed to remove before weighting.
choices.rationale: Stopwords like "the" appear in nearly every document, so their IDF is close to zero and their TF-IDF weights are low even without explicit removal. A high score signals the opposite of stopword behavior.

choices.choice_text: The term's vector is semantically close to the article's other terms in embedding space.
choices.rationale: TF-IDF is a count-based weighting scheme with no notion of semantic similarity or embeddings. It cannot tell whether terms are related in meaning, only how their occurrence is distributed across documents.

mcqQuestions.question_text: Comparing two writing samples, an analyst finds Sample A repeats a small vocabulary inside long, deeply nested sentences, while Sample B uses varied vocabulary in short, simple sentences. How do standard text statistics characterize the samples?

choices.choice_text: Sample A has high lexical diversity because its long sentences contain more total words.
choices.rationale: This conflates token count with diversity. Lexical diversity measures like type-token ratio compare unique words to total words, so repeating a small vocabulary over many tokens yields a low score, not a high one.

choices.choice_text: Sample A shows low lexical diversity but high syntactic complexity; Sample B shows the reverse.
choices.rationale: Correct. Lexical diversity (e.g., type-token ratio) reflects vocabulary variety, which is low for A's repeated words and high for B; syntactic complexity (e.g., clause nesting, parse depth) reflects sentence structure, which is high for A and low for B.

choices.choice_text: Sample B has high syntactic complexity because varied, rarer vocabulary makes its sentences more sophisticated.
choices.rationale: This conflates word choice with sentence structure. Syntactic complexity is computed from structural features like clause embedding and parse-tree depth, so B's short, simple sentences score low regardless of how varied its vocabulary is.

choices.choice_text: The two measures move together, so whichever sample scores higher on one also scores higher on the other.
choices.rationale: Lexical diversity and syntactic complexity capture independent dimensions of text, and this very scenario shows them diverging: a writer can repeat words in elaborate structures or vary words in plain structures.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A data scientist is training a byte-pair encoding (BPE) tokenizer from scratch on a domain-specific corpus. Which process does the BPE algorithm use to build its subword vocabulary?

choices.choice_text: It starts from individual characters and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent symbol pair until the target vocabulary size is reached.
choices.rationale: This is exactly how BPE works: it begins with a base alphabet of characters (or bytes), counts adjacent symbol pairs in the corpus, merges the most frequent pair into a new token, and repeats until the vocabulary hits the desired size. The learned merge rules are then applied in order at tokenization time.

choices.choice_text: It splits each word at linguistically defined morpheme boundaries taken from a curated dictionary of prefixes, roots, and suffixes.
choices.rationale: This reflects the misconception that subword units come from linguistic knowledge. BPE is purely statistical and frequency-driven; its merges often align loosely with morphemes but no dictionary or linguistic analysis is involved.

choices.choice_text: It trains a unigram language model over a large pool of candidate subwords and prunes the tokens that contribute least to corpus likelihood.
choices.rationale: This describes the Unigram tokenization algorithm (a top-down, prune-based method popularized by SentencePiece), not BPE. BPE builds its vocabulary bottom-up through greedy pair merges rather than by pruning a large candidate set.

choices.choice_text: It adds every whole word to the vocabulary and maps any word below a frequency threshold to a single unknown token.
choices.rationale: This is word-level tokenization, the approach BPE was designed to replace. BPE avoids huge word vocabularies and unknown-token replacement by composing words from reusable subword units.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A team is training one tokenizer for a multilingual corpus that includes Japanese and Thai, languages written without spaces between words. Why is SentencePiece well suited to this corpus?

choices.choice_text: It treats raw text as a symbol stream, encoding whitespace like any other character, so no language-specific pre-tokenization is required.
choices.rationale: SentencePiece learns subwords directly from raw sentences and represents whitespace as an ordinary symbol (the ▁ marker), so it never needs the text split into words first. This makes it language-agnostic and directly applicable to scripts that lack word delimiters.

choices.choice_text: It ships with dedicated word-segmentation models for each supported language that split every sentence into words before any subword learning begins.
choices.rationale: This is the opposite of SentencePiece's design goal: it exists precisely to avoid external, language-specific segmenters. Requiring a per-language word segmenter is the limitation of whitespace-dependent tokenizers that SentencePiece removes.

choices.choice_text: It transliterates every script into a shared phonetic alphabet so all languages can reuse one whitespace-delimited vocabulary.
choices.rationale: This reflects a misconception that multilingual tokenizers normalize scripts to a common romanized form. SentencePiece operates on the original characters (or bytes) of each script and performs no transliteration.

choices.choice_text: It stores a complete word-level dictionary covering every language observed in the training corpus.
choices.rationale: A full multilingual word dictionary would be enormous and would still fail on unseen words, which is why subword methods exist. SentencePiece learns a compact subword vocabulary rather than enumerating whole words per language.

mcqQuestions.question_text: At inference time, a chatbot receives the invented brand name 'Zorvexil', which never appeared in the tokenizer's training data. How does a subword tokenizer such as BPE typically handle this word?

choices.choice_text: It decomposes the word into smaller subword or character-level pieces that already exist in its vocabulary.
choices.rationale: Subword tokenizers can back off to progressively smaller units, ultimately individual characters or bytes, so any string can be represented from known vocabulary entries. This is the key property that makes true out-of-vocabulary failures rare with subword methods.

choices.choice_text: It maps the entire word to a single unknown token, discarding all information about its internal spelling.
choices.rationale: Wholesale replacement with an [UNK] token is the behavior of word-level tokenizers with closed vocabularies. Subword tokenization was adopted largely to avoid this information loss by composing rare words from known pieces.

choices.choice_text: It appends the new word to its vocabulary at inference time and initializes a fresh embedding row so the model can represent it.
choices.rationale: This reflects the misconception that vocabularies grow dynamically. The tokenizer vocabulary and the model's embedding matrix are fixed after training; adding entries at inference would break the correspondence between token IDs and learned embeddings.

choices.choice_text: It drops the unrecognized word and tokenizes only the surrounding text it recognizes from training.
choices.rationale: Tokenizers do not silently delete input; every span of text is mapped to some token sequence. Dropping content would corrupt the model's view of the user's message, and subword back-off makes it unnecessary.

mcqQuestions.question_text: An engineer implements semantic search by comparing sentence embeddings and selects cosine similarity instead of raw dot product for ranking. What property of cosine similarity motivates this choice?

choices.choice_text: It ranks vectors by their angle, so magnitude differences unrelated to meaning do not distort similarity scores.
choices.rationale: Cosine similarity normalizes both vectors, measuring only their directional alignment in embedding space. Embedding norms can vary with factors like text length or token frequency rather than semantics, so ignoring magnitude tends to produce more meaning-driven rankings.

choices.choice_text: It is significantly cheaper to compute than a dot product when searching across a large index of embeddings.
choices.rationale: Cosine similarity is a dot product plus normalization by the two vector norms, so it is marginally more work, not less. The motivation is score quality and comparability, not compute savings.

choices.choice_text: It preserves information about word order within each sentence, which a raw dot-product comparison between vectors discards.
choices.rationale: Neither similarity function sees word order; both operate on fixed-length vectors after encoding. Any word-order sensitivity comes from the embedding model itself, not from the choice of similarity metric.

choices.choice_text: It returns calibrated relevance probabilities instead of unbounded scores that are hard to threshold.
choices.rationale: Cosine similarity is bounded in [-1, 1], but bounded scores are not calibrated probabilities of relevance. Treating cosine scores as probabilities is a common misreading of what the metric provides.

mcqQuestions.question_text: A search team using static word2vec embeddings finds the query 'bank deposit' also retrieves documents about river banks. Why would switching to contextual embeddings from a BERT-style model address this problem?

choices.choice_text: They produce a distinct vector for each occurrence of 'bank', conditioned on the surrounding words in the text.
choices.rationale: Contextual models compute each token's representation from its entire surrounding context via self-attention, so 'bank' near 'deposit' and 'bank' near 'river' receive different vectors. Static embeddings like word2vec assign one fixed vector per word type, blending all senses together.

choices.choice_text: They use an expanded vocabulary in which each sense of a polysemous word receives its own dedicated token entry.
choices.rationale: This reflects the misconception that sense disambiguation is solved in the vocabulary. BERT-style models typically use a single subword entry for 'bank'; the sense separation emerges from context-dependent computation, not from per-sense tokens.

choices.choice_text: They are trained on much larger corpora, so the financial sense comes to dominate the single vector assigned to 'bank'.
choices.rationale: More data does not fix polysemy if the model still produces one vector per word type; a dominant sense would simply make the minority sense retrieve worse. The fix comes from per-occurrence, context-conditioned representations, not corpus scale.

choices.choice_text: They discard rare senses of each word during pre-training, keeping only its most frequent meaning.
choices.rationale: Contextual models retain the ability to represent all senses; nothing is pruned during pre-training. Dropping minority senses would harm understanding rather than improve retrieval for queries that need the frequent sense.

mcqQuestions.objective_title: Text data cleaning and feature engineering
mcqQuestions.question_text: A team is preparing a large web-scraped corpus to pre-train an LLM. Many documents appear multiple times, and some near-duplicates also exist in the held-out evaluation set. Why is deduplicating the corpus before training important?

choices.choice_text: It reduces verbatim memorization of repeated text and prevents train-test overlap from inflating evaluation scores.
choices.rationale: Correct. Repeated documents are memorized more readily by LLMs, and duplicates shared with the evaluation set constitute leakage, making benchmark scores look better than true generalization. Deduplication addresses both problems.

choices.choice_text: It primarily lowers storage and compute costs; repeated training examples otherwise have no meaningful effect on final model quality.
choices.rationale: Misconception that deduplication is only an efficiency step. Duplicates do affect quality: they promote memorization and, when shared with evaluation data, bias measured performance upward.

choices.choice_text: It prevents the tokenizer from assigning duplicated documents their own dedicated vocabulary entries.
choices.rationale: Subword tokenizers build vocabularies from frequent character sequences, not from whole documents. Duplicates can skew frequency statistics slightly, but tokenizers never create per-document entries, so this is not the rationale.

choices.choice_text: It stops gradients from exploding, because batches containing duplicates destabilize the optimizer.
choices.rationale: Confuses a data-quality issue with a numerical-stability issue. Gradient explosion relates to learning rate, initialization, and architecture; duplicated examples do not by themselves destabilize optimization.

mcqQuestions.objective_title: Text data cleaning and feature engineering
mcqQuestions.question_text: To expand a small sentiment-classification training set, an engineer generates new examples with back-translation and synonym replacement, copying each source example's label onto its augmented version. What is the main risk of this approach?

choices.choice_text: The augmented text can shift in meaning or sentiment, so the copied label no longer fits.
choices.rationale: Correct. Back-translation and synonym swaps can subtly alter semantics (e.g., replacing a word with a synonym of different polarity), producing label noise when the original label is reused unchanged.

choices.choice_text: Enlarging the training set with automatically augmented examples directly increases the model's tendency to overfit the training data.
choices.rationale: Reverses the usual effect: augmentation typically acts as a regularizer and reduces overfitting by adding variation. The real hazard is label-text mismatch, not dataset size.

choices.choice_text: Back-translation copies evaluation examples into the training split, causing test-set leakage.
choices.rationale: Confuses augmentation with data-splitting hygiene. Back-translation rewrites existing training examples; it does not touch the evaluation split, so it cannot by itself introduce leakage.

choices.choice_text: Synonym replacement changes sequence lengths, which transformer classifiers cannot process.
choices.rationale: Transformers handle variable-length inputs routinely via padding and attention masks. Small length changes from word substitutions pose no processing problem.

mcqQuestions.objective_title: Text data cleaning and feature engineering
mcqQuestions.question_text: Before fine-tuning an LLM on a newly collected instruction dataset, a data scientist wants a first-pass exploratory analysis that catches problems known to degrade fine-tuned models. Which set of checks is most useful to run first?

choices.choice_text: Examine text-length distributions, label or category balance, and duplicate examples.
choices.rationale: Correct. These EDA checks surface the most common dataset defects before training: sequences that exceed the context window, skewed categories that bias the model, and duplicates that cause memorization or split leakage.

choices.choice_text: Sweep learning rates and batch sizes to identify the strongest fine-tuning configuration.
choices.rationale: This is hyperparameter tuning, not exploratory data analysis. It optimizes training on whatever data exists but cannot reveal defects in the dataset itself.

choices.choice_text: Visualize the base model's attention maps to see which input tokens it attends to.
choices.rationale: Attention visualization is a model-interpretability technique; it inspects model behavior, not dataset quality, and would not expose length outliers, imbalance, or duplication.

choices.choice_text: Profile GPU memory utilization to confirm the dataset fits on the training hardware.
